narrativeText: How many of these organisms will be extinct 500 years from now? Someday, the specimens you see here and in other museums may be all that's left of these threatened species. | A natural history museum is a time machine that people in the future will visit and use to study the biodiversity of the past.

Contains 9 specimens across diverse taxa: one sea turtle (Eretmochelys imbricata, customs seizure ST-37), one sponge (Porifera, MI4236), four species of land snails (Papuina pulcherrima MI4071 d-g, Helicostyla cincinniformis MI4232 a-d, Helicostyla florida MI4234, Liguus virgineus MI4172 c-f), one placostyle snail (Placostylus sp. MI4235), one freshwater mussel (unionid, MI4233), and one fern (Woodwardia fimbriata E0147). Two narrative text blocks address species extinction and the role of museums. narrativeText: The specimens in our collections have come from researchers, private collectors and hunters, customs agencies, zoos, and others over the past century. Today, we actively take only invertebrates, plants, and fungi for our collections. Some fish and, rarely, birds are still deposited here after being used for research. All the mammals, birds, reptiles, and amphibians that come into the museum today are salvaged — from roadkill and window collisions, for example.
notes: 5 chipmunk study skins displayed. Specimen details: 1. July 31, 1947, Malachi, Ontario, No. 5592. 2. July 31, 1946, Black Sturgeon Lake, Ontario, No. 2879. 3. August 2, 1947, Marten River, Nipissing District, Ontario, No. 2880. 4. July 26, 1945, Kawene, Ontario, No. 4026. 5. July 20, 1946, Black Sturgeon Lake, Ontario, No. 5591. Photo dimensions noted as 15.5" x 10".

narrativeText: This white sweet clover, collected along a Yukon highway, could take over native grasslands. Historical specimens from the herbarium are helping scientists understand how this plant, introduced from Europe to improve farm soils, is spreading over time and affecting native species.
notes: Sweet clover: August 5, 2010, Km 305, Klondike Highway, Yukon. Smooth brome: August 5, 2010, Km 253, Klondike Highway, Yukon. Narrowleaf hawksbeard: August 5, 2010, Km 390, Klondike Highway, Yukon. Map shows Alaska, Dawson City, Whitehorse, British Columbia. Researcher profile: Jamie Leathem, University of British Columbia, 2010 - graduate student in UBC's Department of Botany researching whether species could become invasive in the future.

narrativeText: “In all works on Natural History, we constantly find details of the marvellous adaptation of animals to their food, their habits, and the localities in which they are found.” — Alfred Russel Wallace, A Narrative of Travels on the Amazon and Rio Negro, 1853
notes: Quote-only panel, no specimens displayed. Attribution to Alfred Russel Wallace, 1853.

narrativeText: Why would researchers kill these insects and fish? Specimens like these let us study an organism’s hidden structures and DNA. We can even use the chemistry of their tissues to learn about what they ate and where they lived. The more we learn about a species, the better prepared we are to conserve it. UBC’s research on these fish has helped protection efforts under Canada’s Species at Risk Act.
notes: Mountain suckers: August 8, 2009, Similkameen River, near Keremeos, BC. Fish shown in glass vials, photo dimensions 9" x 6.5". Wide-stripe grass-veneer moth: June 19, 2010, UBC campus, Vancouver, BC. Honeybee: June 17, 2010, UBC campus, Vancouver, BC. Dampwood termite: August 21, 2010, Katherine Lake Campground, Garden City, BC. Insect photo dimensions 3" x 7".

narrativeText: While studying the genes of a California tarweed, UBC researchers discovered that what everyone thought was one species was actually two. Looking closer, they found that even white-flowered and yellow-flowered versions that grow next to each other don't interbreed, which confirms they're separate species.
notes: Three herbarium specimens of Calycadenia pauciflora showing yellow and white flower forms. Researcher profile: Jeannette Whitton, University of British Columbia, 2010 - associate professor in UBC's Department of Botany and the director of the Herbarium, researches plant systematics and evolution.

narrativeText: "The affinities of all the beings of the same class have sometimes been represented by a great tree... As buds give rise by growth to fresh buds, and these if vigorous, branch out and overtop on all sides many a feebler branch, so by generation I believe it has been with the great Tree of Life, which fills with its dead and broken branches the crust of the earth, and covers the surface with its ever branching and beautiful ramifications." —Charles Darwin, On the Origin of Species, 1859
legibility: mostly legible; some small labels partially obscured
notes: Large Tree of Life display panel (not a traditional drawer). Features diverse specimens across kingdoms. Metallic wood-boring beetle labeled as family Buprestidae, 1966-67, Chiang Mai, Thailand. Southern baler from Western Australia. Musk mallow collected July 2010 at intersection of SW Marine Drive and 16th Avenue, Vancouver, BC. Yellow-crowned Amazon from World Parrot Refuge, Coombs, BC. Background shows Darwin's Tree of Life diagram with Roman numerals and letter/number grid.

narrativeText: Record, record, record! Write down as much as you can when in the field: the date, location, collector’s name, and habitat information. These specimens are labelled with numbers that correspond to entries in this naturalist’s journal. Because of these records, we know where and how these century-old specimens lived.
legibility: good; display labels clear, journal handwriting partially legible
notes: Display panel about field recording. Features Hook field journal (open) alongside pinned insect specimens. Longhorn beetle family Cerambycidae, all specimens dated July 22, 1910, Yokohama, Japan. Beetle (No. 810) labeled as Coleoptera. Butterfly labeled as Nymphalidae. Moth labeled as Drepanidae. Journal entries reference locations in BC including Kelowna and entries from 1913-1914.

narrativeText: To last for hundreds of years, bird skins must be handled carefully. The compact shape of study skins like these helps prevent delicate feathers and skin from being damaged when researchers study the birds. To better understand modern ecosystems, researchers uncover their past. Louise Blight analyses museum specimens dating from 1860 to find chemical changes in seabird feathers and fish muscles over time. These changes reveal alterations to marine food webs from fishing and climate change. Researchers often use seabirds as ecosystem monitors because they’re common and widespread.
notes: Display panel about bird skin preparation and study skins. Shows three stages: bird cut open and turned inside out, cleaned skin stuffed with cotton and sewn up, wrapping and pinning to dry. Varied thrush No. E.0400: November 22, 2010, Surrey, BC. No. E.0401: December 4, 2010, North Vancouver, BC. No. E.0402: November 23, 2010, Vancouver, BC. Glaucous-winged gull: February 5, 1971, Tsawwassen, BC. Features researcher profile: Louise Blight, PhD candidate, UBC Centre for Applied Conservation Research, University of British Columbia, 2007. Panel code D122.

narrativeText: We use delicate techniques for delicate specimens. Insects are dried and mounted on pins, and tiny labels attached. Sealed in boxes to keep out pests, specimens can last for hundreds of years.
legibility: narrative text clear; individual specimen pin labels in Schmidt box too small to read
notes: Display panel about insect specimen preparation techniques. Shows Schmidt box with approximately 8-10 pinned insect specimens (labels illegible at this resolution), insect pins (D.F. Tayler & Co., New Hall Works, Birmingham, Patent Entomological Pins), insect kill jar, alcohol vials, and insect spreading board and pins (stamped Spencer Entomological Museum, Department of Zoology, University of British Columbia) with a butterfly/moth being spread. Panel code D011.

narrativeText: Some soft-bodied specimens, like fish, marine invertebrates, and spiders, are preserved in fluid to keep them intact and retain their shape. Most fish are “fixed” in formaldehyde first, which solidifies their original shape, then stored in alcohol in clear containers. It’s an old method, but a good one: specimens preserved in “spirits of wine” as early as the 1600s are still around today.
notes: Display panel about wet/fluid preservation. Ten specimens in vials arranged in two rows. Top row L-R: Kelp isopod (July 3 2010, Botanical Beach, BC), Gooseneck barnacle (July 3, 2010, Botanical Beach, BC), Oregon spotted frog (captive-bred, Abbotsford, BC), Stickleback (Gasterosteidae, August 22, 2010, Katherine Lake Park, BC), Dead man's fingers (August 22, 2010, Skookumchuck Narrows Provincial Park, BC). Bottom row L-R: Hermit crab (Paguroidea, July 3, 2010, Botanical Beach, BC), Limpet (Botanical Beach, BC, July 3, 2010), Termite (September 9, 2009, Jericho Beach, Vancouver, BC), Coho salmon, Goby (Gobiidae, May 13, 2010, Spanish Banks intertidal area, Vancouver, BC). Panel code D012.

narrativeText: Preparing plants for a museum begins with collecting a plant specimen in the field and recording as much information as possible. Next comes drying and pressing, which can take up to four weeks. Then plants are frozen to kill pests. Vascular plants and algae are then mounted on archival paper with labels, while mosses and fungi are stored in envelopes.
notes: Display panel about plant specimen preparation. Shows pressed Salal specimen mounted on archival paper (October 24, 2010, Vancouver, BC), Bog moss in envelope (July 31, 2010, Cypress Provincial Park, BC), archival tape, and archival glue bottle. Panel code D013.

narrativeText: Unearthing fossils is a bit like very careful mining with small miner’s tools—hammers, rock picks, trowels, and screens. Back in the lab, some fossils are cleaned with water; others are further uncovered by a small sandblaster that erodes away soft rock around the fossil.
notes: Display panel about fossil excavation and preparation. Shows trilobite (pyrite replacement), Cambrian, Antelope Springs, Utah. Photo of fossil excavation in Drummond, Montana (credit: Craig Christensen). Tools displayed: chisel kit, trowel, brush, ruler. Panel code D014.

narrativeText: Want to discover a new species? Biologists have travelled the world to do just that, but you don't need to. Choose a patch of ground in a local park and scoop up some soil in a cup. Millions upon millions of microbes live in a soil sample, from bacteria to viruses to fungi to protists—most of which have never been identified. We have discovered only a fraction of Earth's species. Biodiversity hot spots—areas likely to support myriad life forms—are where many scientists look for unclassified species. A magnifying glass can help you discover new species of fungi, roundworms, and arthropods in your neighbourhood. Diverse bacteria growing on the surface of a microbial eukaryote. Examine a speck of soil under a microscope or using DNA sequencing, and you're bound to find a new microbial species. Healthy coastal seas, whether in the tropics or temperate regions, are teeming with life and diversity. Go snorkelling, scuba diving or beachcombing, and you'll be rewarded with a visual feast. Open the drawers to learn more about these fascinating ecosystems.
notes: Interpretive panel about discovering new species. Features world map with biodiversity hotspots in green, soil sample display, magnifying glass, microscope, and electron microscope image of bacteria. Three insect specimens with labels. Additional labels: Dampwood termite collected August 21, 2010 at Katherine Lake Campground, Garden City, BC; Bush cricket collected August 13, 2010 at Deas Island Park, Richmond, BC; Common European earwig collected July 16, 2010 at Pacific Spirit Park, Vancouver, BC. Reference code D022 visible near bottom-center icons. Temperate/Tropical divider line visible.

narrativeText: The microscope revolutionized biology by revealing a world invisible to the human eye. It made possible some of the most important breakthroughs in biology, starting with the discovery that all life is made up of cells, and that many living things are single cells. Microscopy has since revealed an entire universe of these microorganisms, which play a critical role in sustaining all life on Earth. In 1665, Robert C. Hooke discovered plant cells while using a microscope to magnify a slice of cork. Using his handcrafted microscopes, Antonie van Leeuwenhoek discovered the first microorganisms.
notes: Interpretive panel about the history of microscopy. Features replica of van Leeuwenhoek microscope made by Patrick Keeling with diagram showing eye-microscope-candle alignment. Includes illustration of Robert C. Hooke's microscope from Micrographia, 1665; cork cells and mimosa leaves by Robert C. Hooke, Micrographia, 1665; Portrait of Antonie van Leeuwenhoek (1632–1723) by Jan Verkolje. Actual microscope replica displayed (labeled 2.5" x 6"). Panel label D016 visible.

narrativeText: Where are you on the evolutionary tree of life? Your microscopic relatives would place you on a side branch. In the 1970s, DNA sequencing revealed a microbial world whose diversity far surpassed that of plants, animals, and fungi. This led to a redrawing of the tree of life and ushered in a new era of microbiology. The microscopic, hollow wires of this component of a DNA sequencing machine force pieces of dyed DNA to pass through in order of size. The machines generate images where each colour represents one of the four nucleotides that make up DNA.
notes: Interpretive panel about the tree of life and DNA sequencing. Features Modern tree of life (2004) showing Eukaryotes, Archaea, and Bacteria domains, and Traditional tree of life (1874) showing Haeckel's 'Pedigree of Man' (Plate XV). Includes a displayed component of a DNA sequencing machine and a DNA sequencing output image. Panel label D017 visible.

narrativeText: Wilf Schofield spent nearly 40 years studying mosses, building up one of the biggest collections in the world at this museum. Between his arrival at UBC in 1960 and his death in 2008, he expanded the collection from 3,000 to 300,000 specimens. He worked in the field up to the month before his passing, actively conducting research, writing scholarly texts, teaching, and mentoring. Dr. Wilf Schofield was the curator of bryophytes at the UBC herbarium. His love of plants fuelled a lifetime dedication to the study of mosses.
notes: Interpretive panel about Dr. Wilf Schofield, bryophyte curator at UBC. Displays his personal field equipment: specimen scraper, newspaper envelopes, field hat (Lubrecht Forest cap), collection bags, and shoulder bag. Photo caption: University of British Columbia, 1960–2008. Envelope stamped L03 with date 12/2/01. Panel label D018 visible.

narrativeText: Imagine the feeling of finding a new species that no scientist has ever seen before. What questions might you want to ask about this new form of life? Deep in the jungles of Papua New Guinea, Wayne Maddison discovered dozens of new species of jumping spiders within three weeks. His research has taken him to six continents, but he says there are probably undescribed spiders right here in Vancouver. Wayne Maddison is the co-director of the Beaty Biodiversity Museum and a professor and Canada Research Chair in UBC's Departments of Zoology and Botany. He has studied jumping spiders around the world for over 40 years. These are just some of the places Wayne Maddison has collected. There are too many to put on a map!
notes: Interpretive panel about Wayne Maddison and jumping spider discovery. Features large macro photo of jumping spider, specimen in vial under magnifying lens, world map with pink dots showing collection locations, and photo of Maddison (University of British Columbia, 2010). Specimen collected August 4, 2008 at Varirata National Park, Papua New Guinea. Panel label D019 visible.

narrativeText: What could be more exciting than discovering a new species? How about finding one that everyone thinks is extinct? On an expedition to a remote South Atlantic island, Quentin Cronk and his team rediscovered the St. Helena ebony tree, last seen in 1860 and believed extinct. The two plants he found have been used to successfully reintroduce the species. The first St. Helena ebony flower seen for over 100 years. Introduced goats had destroyed the trees' native habitat. Luckily, the goats couldn't reach this cliffside, where a few survivors were growing. Quentin Cronk is a professor of plant science at UBC and the herbarium's curator of eudicots. He studies the evolution of flowers, leaves, and plant development. Northern and southern resident orcas eat fish, while transient orcas eat marine mammals.
notes: Interpretive panel about rediscovery of the St. Helena ebony tree by Quentin Cronk. Specimen is a subfossil collected 1980 near Asses Ears, St. Helena, South Atlantic Ocean. Features photo of St. Helena ebony flower, cliffside photo with annotations showing George Benjamin (holding rope), Charlie Benjamin, Ebony plant No. 1, and Ebony plant No. 2. Photo of Quentin Cronk (University of British Columbia, 2010). Panel label D020 visible.

narrativeText: Sometimes you can discover a new species just by watching how animals behave. British Columbia's orcas all look similar and live in the same waters, but they're actually three different assemblages that have different feeding and travel habits and use different sounds to communicate. DNA analysis shows that they are well on their way to becoming separate species. Dr. Lance Barrett-Lennard is an adjunct professor in UBC's Department of Zoology and a researcher at the Vancouver Aquarium Marine Science Centre. He studies the genetics of orca populations on the BC coast. Northern and southern resident orcas eat fish, while transient orcas eat marine mammals.
notes: Interpretive panel about BC orca populations and Dr. Lance Barrett-Lennard. Specimen is an orca lower jawbone, labeled 'To be accessioned' (no catalog number). Features maps of British Columbia showing Transient range, Northern resident range, Southern resident range, and Northern and southern overlap. Photo of Barrett-Lennard (University of British Columbia, 2010). Panel label D021 visible.

narrativeText: Healthy coastal seas, whether in the tropics or temperate regions, are teeming with life and diversity. Go snorkelling, scuba diving or beachcombing, and you'll be rewarded with a visual feast. Open the drawers to learn more about these fascinating ecosystems.
notes: Introductory panel for coastal seas exhibit. Two sections divided by vertical dotted line labeled 'Temperate' (left) and 'Tropical' (right). No specimens are present in this panel — it is purely text and design. Previous extraction incorrectly listed a moth specimen (cat: 803); this has been removed.

narrativeText: Between the high and low tide lines lies the intertidal zone: a place where marine life clings to the shore in horizontal bands. Heat and dryness help set species' upper limits, and interactions like competition and predation set the lower limits. The low shore is very diverse, while the high shore supports only a few, hardy species.
notes: COMPLETE RE-EXTRACTION. Previous extraction was entirely wrong (described insect specimen preparation). This is an intertidal zone display organized by three zones: High tide zone (barnacles, crabs, sea snails — species tolerate dryness and extreme temperatures), Middle tide zone (black turban, file dogwinkle shell, chiton — marine life can handle regular submersion and exposure), Low tide zone (frilled dogwinkle, Oregon rock crab, sea urchin — usually underwater, tolerate only brief periods of dryness). Features researcher profile: Rebecca Kordas, PhD candidate, UBC Department of Zoology, 2010, studying ecological impacts of climate warming on intertidal habitats. Photo credit: Mark Wilson. Scale bar: 1 inch.

narrativeText: Shoreline species have evolved to meet the challenges of life in the intertidal zone. Crabs hide from the sun and keep moist under rocks and seaweed. Mussels and barnacles hold in sea water to avoid drying out, and some seaweed gets brittle without dying. Kelp anchors itself to the seabed and most molluscs have shells to withstand crashing waves. Strong shells and foot suction allow limpets to resist prying predators, like crabs.
notes: COMPLETE RE-EXTRACTION. Previous extraction was entirely wrong (described Ordovician fossils: bryozoans, brachiopods, graptolites, black pepper). This is actually a shoreline species adaptation display focused on limpets (family Acmaeidae). Shows multiple limpet shell specimens of varying sizes and patterns. Includes a large photo of barnacles and limpets on rocks (credit: Mark Wilson). No individual catalog numbers visible for the limpet specimens. Scale bar: 1 inch. Label reads 'Limpets / Acmaeidae'.

narrativeText: One species helping another without harm to itself is called facilitation. More mussels mean more diversity in the intertidal zone. They push out competitors, like seaweed and barnacles, to form mussel beds, which provide living space for millions of tiny marine animals. These mussel-bed guests also gain refuge from certain predators.
notes: CORRECTED: Previous extraction was entirely wrong (listed Salal, Yellow warbler, Spotted towhee, insect orders panel). Actual image is a single text panel about facilitation ecology in mussel beds. Features researcher Gerald Singh (PhD candidate, UBC Institute for Resources, Environment, and Sustainability, 2010), researching mussel bed productivity where sea otters are being reintroduced. Photo credit: Aaron Tam. No specimen labels or species identifiers present in this image.

narrativeText: Every type of hard or soft coral, from tropical reef-builders to cold-water dwellers, is a community of tiny polyps, related to anemones. Most tropical coral polyps harbour microscopic algae called zooxanthellae (zo-zan-thell-ee), which convert sunlight into nutrients that help corals form hard skeletons. Cold-water corals often survive without them.
notes: CORRECTED: Previous extraction was entirely wrong (listed 16 crop/seed species: flax, oat, beans, wheat, etc.). Actual image is a coral diversity display panel with 12 labeled coral and gorgonian specimens. Coral polyp illustration credited to Segal Friedmann. The Primnoa and Balanophyllia share a catalog number as they are on the same rock.

narrativeText: Thank a pollinator for every third bite of food you eat. Pollination by insects, birds, and bats is responsible for about 30 per cent of the world's food supply. When crops are exposed to a variety and abundance of pollinators, food production soars. More pollinators equals more berries, fruits, vegetables, nuts, and even chocolate to satisfy your palate.
notes: Large composite image. Crops show a pollination and food display (D166). Sunflower illustration by Sigal Friedmann. Seed/food specimens in circular containers with handwritten labels including family names: Sesame (F. Pedaliaceae), Sunflower seeds (F. Asteraceae), Coffee beans (F. Rubiaceae), Mustard seeds (F. Brassicaceae), Brazil nuts (F. Lecythidaceae), Allspice seeds (F. Myrtaceae). Also displayed without scientific labels: Hazelnut shells, Pecans, Almond shells, Walnut shells, Watermelon seeds (partially visible), Chocolate (Lindt Excellence 90% Cacao Dark-Noir bar).

narrativeText: Tropical corals need coastal mangrove forests. Mangroves clean water flowing from the land, and nearby seagrass beds provide nursery habitat for baby fish, invertebrate animals, and other reef life. When mangroves are destroyed by coastal development, marine life suffers and corals die from cloudy, polluted waters. One-third of all corals and the animals that depend on them are endangered.
notes: CORRECTED: (1) 'Chaetopleura angulata / Marble chitonids' was wrong -- actual specimen is Clanculus pharaonius / Mantle clanculus (MI4097 a-f). (2) Resolved 4 NULL scientificNames: Hippocampus spp. (seahorses), Nerita peloronta (bleeding tooth nerite), Mitra mitra (episcopal mitre), Lambis scorpius (scorpio conch -- was listed as 'Sponge conch', corrected to 'Scorpio conch'). (3) 'Angaria delphinus' corrected to Angaria melanacantha. (4) 'Orthoeciadae sp.' (vase sponge) corrected to Cribrochalina sp. (6) The unlabeled white branching coral specimen visible in upper-right coral reef zone has no label in the image. Display spans mangrove forest, seagrass beds, and coral reef ecological zones.

narrativeText: What are marine invasive species? They're marine animals brought into Canada from other parts of the world, intentionally or by accident. Along British Columbia's coasts, some marine invaders are so common, you'd swear they belong here. Do you know which of these marine animals are native to British Columbia and which were introduced? It's a trick question: they're all invaders! They arrived in ships' ballast water, clinging to boat hulls, or as hitchhikers in the live food trade.
notes: Display panel about marine invasive species in British Columbia. Includes a map of British Columbia. Also visible in zoomed crops: a separate display showing a Hook field journal (open book with handwritten entries), and pinned insect specimens — a longhorn beetle (Cerambycidae, July 22, 1910, Yokohama, Japan, No. 804), a beetle (Cetonia sp., July 22, 1910, Yokohama, Japan, No. 798), a beetle (Coleoptera, July 22, 1910, Yokohama, Japan, No. 810), a butterfly (Nymphalidae, July 22, 1910, Yokohama, Japan, No. 806), and a moth (Drepanidae, July 22, 1910, Yokohama, Japan, No. 803). Text reads: 'Record, record, record! Write down as much as you can when in the field: the date, location, collector's name, and habitat information. These specimens are labelled with numbers that correspond to entries in this naturalist's journal. Because of these records, we know where and how these century-old specimens lived.' Display code D009 visible.

narrativeText: The European green crab is listed as one of the 100 worst invasive species in the world. Since 1999, along the west coast of Vancouver Island, this hardy crab has been competing with native crabs for food and resources. It also preys on native clams, mussels, and young crabs. Its colour can vary. To identify it properly, look for five spines on each side of its eyes.
notes: Display panel focused on the European green crab. Includes photo of researcher. Text: 'Megan E. Mach is a PhD student at UBC's Institute for Resources, Environment and Sustainability. She is examining potential impacts of the European green crab.' University of British Columbia, 2009. Also visible in zoomed crops: a separate 'Tree of Life' display with Darwin quote from On the Origin of Species (1859), specimens including Southern baler (Melo miltonis, Western Australia, No. M14149), Trametes versicolor (No. E.0242), Polyporus elegans (No. E.0240 a,b), Pycnoporellus fibrillosus (No. E.0241 a-c), Gomphus floccosus (No. E.0339 a-c), Musk mallow (Malva moschata, July 2010, collected roadside at intersection of SW Marine Drive and 16th Avenue, Vancouver, BC, No. E.0301), Yellow-crowned Amazon (Amazona ochrocephala, World Parrot Refuge, Coombs, BC, No. WCY10), Metallic wood-boring beetle (Buprestidae, 1966-67, Chiang Mai, Thailand, No. E.0100). Display code D078 visible.

narrativeText: The discovery of common periwinkles in Stanley Park was an important find, because these large marine snails could alter British Columbia's shorelines. They crowd out native snails and devour algae, undermining the foundation of intertidal ecosystems. The common periwinkle has a smooth, onion-shaped shell. At up to 2.5 centimetres in diameter, it is larger than any native periwinkle.
notes: Display panel comparing one invasive periwinkle species (Common periwinkle, Littorina littorea) to three native species (Checkered, Sitka, Raven). Includes map of Stanley Park and Vancouver. Researcher text: 'Chris Harley is an associate professor in UBC's Department of Zoology. He and his students at UBC discovered common periwinkles in Stanley Park in 2010.' University of British Columbia, 2010. Display code D031 visible.

narrativeText: "Sea squirt" may be a cute name, but don't be fooled by these invasive animals. These tunicates should be arrested for stealing vital space and food from native ones. Free from their usual predators, invasive sea squirts grow and multiply quickly — suffocating other marine life. Invasive sea squirts in British Columbia clog up aquaculture equipment.
notes: Display panel about invasive tunicates. Researcher text: 'Cathryn Clarke Murray is a PhD candidate at UBC. She is a marine ecologist studying sea squirts and other invasive species to determine the extent and mechanisms of invasion in British Columbia.' Also visible in zoomed crops: a separate display about entomological techniques, titled 'Delicate techniques for delicate specimens. Insects are dried and mounted on pins, and tiny labels attached. Sealed in boxes to keep out pests, specimens can last for hundreds of years.' Shows a Schmidt box with pinned beetles, insect kill jar, alcohol vials, insect spreading board and pins, D.F. Tayler & Co. (New Hall Works, Birmingham) Patent Entomological Pins box. Wooden box stamped 'SPENCER ENTOMOLOGICAL MUSEUM DEPARTMENT OF ZOOLOGY UNIVERSITY OF BRITISH COLUMBIA'. Display code D011 visible.

narrativeText: Are all introduced species bad? Not always. Manila, varnish, and softshell clams have spread without noticeably harming British Columbia's coastal ecosystems. Accidentally introduced through the oyster trade, they are now harvested commercially and added to chowders, soups, and appetizers — to the delight of many seafood connoisseurs. Notice the distinctive large hinge at the end of the softshell clam's shell. The varnish clam's brittle shell has brown "skin" and a purple interior. To identify the Manila clam, look for the purple stain inside its shell.
notes: Display panel about commercially harvested introduced clams. Researcher text: 'Laura L. F. White is a PhD student at UBC, researching marine invasive shellfish and their impacts on native species in BC and around the world.' Also visible in zoomed crops: a separate display about medicinal plants with pressed botanical specimens on purple circles. Visible specimens include Douglas-fir seed cones (Pseudotsuga menziesii, April 11, 2011, UBC campus, Vancouver, BC, No. E.0852 a-c), pollen cones, Kitchen sage (Salvia officinalis, June 4, 2011, UBC campus Botanical Gardens, Vancouver, BC, No. E.0844), Arnica-leaflike (Eriophyllum lanatum/Epilobium sp., possibly, August 10, 2011, Botanical Gardens, Vancouver, BC, No. E.0892 a,b), Common evening primrose (Oenothera biennis, August 4, 2011, UBC campus Botanical Gardens, Vancouver, BC, No. E.0888). Text about medicinal plants from drug store shelves and Rod of Asclepius symbol visible. Display code D211 visible.

narrativeText: The Pacific oyster has been harvested in British Columbia since 1912 after being imported from Japan. No one expected other marine animals to hitch a ride with the oysters — but they did, including the oyster drill snail. Native Olympia oysters (Ostrea conchaphila) are endangered in British Columbia.
notes: Display panel about the Pacific oyster and oyster drill snail. Researcher text: 'Francis G. Choi is a masters student in UBC's Department of Earth and Ocean Sciences. He is studying the relationship between commercial vessel activities and invasive species in Canadian harbours.' Also visible in zoomed crops: separate display about Kwakwaka'wakw harvesting of alga Porphyra abbottiae on the central BC coast, used with halibut, as medicine for goitre, and traded. Another display about shellfish poisoning with microscopy images of Alexandrium fundyense (photo credit Gary Wikfors/NOAA, 5 micrometer scale bar) and Alexandrium sp. (SEM image, photo credit Steve Morton/NOAA, 5 micrometer scale bar). Text: 'government monitors the safety... to prevent shellfish poisoning... organisms such as these.' Also visible: plant diversity text about samples collected in Vancouver, and a green line drawing. Display codes D188 and D200 visible.

narrativeText: Invasive species threaten biodiversity and cause billions of dollars of damage each year. We need to control them, and scientists are helping. Check out the weird and wonderful ways scientists monitor and gain valuable insights into invasive species.
notes: Display panel showing tools and methods for monitoring invasive species: dive surveys, beach surveys, settlement panels. Shows field equipment including a trowel, garden fork, calipers, quadrat grid, Petri dishes, and photographs of researchers conducting surveys. Also visible in zoomed crops: a separate display about terpenes and Pacific yew. Text: 'Strong aromas like pine, citrus, and mint all come from one group of plant chemicals, called terpenes, often found in tree bark. Many of these chemicals have been shown to ward off insects. Whatever its use to the plant, the terpene taxol, from the bark of Pacific yew, helps fight cancer. Taxol is now synthesized in the lab, sparing wild populations of Pacific yew.' (August 8, 2011, UBC campus, Vancouver, BC, No. E.0573), Pacific yew bark (Taxus brevifolia, August 7, 2011, Emerald Forest Conservation Area, Whistler, BC, No. E.0574). narrativeText: The empty discs on this 'lawn' of bacteria show where researchers placed drops of bacteriophages — viruses that infect bacteria. Kelp is a protist. It's a multi-cellular alga, or brown seaweed, that evolved separately from plants.

narrativeText: This fossilized leaf is not alone. It's surrounded by millions of tiny, fossilized skeletons from protists called "diatoms." Other fossilized protists form many other rocks, including the chalk White Cliffs of Dover and the boulders of the Egyptian pyramids.

narrativeText: Corals, sea slugs, lichens, termites, and cows — what could these animals possibly have in common? They all benefit from protists. Lichens, many corals, and some sea slugs contain algae that are photosynthesizing protists. Termite guts are crammed with protists that digest wood, or cellulose — the termite's main food. Cows and other ruminants have stomachs containing billions of protists, fungi, and bacteria that digest grass and hay.

Cow photograph credited to Ard Hesselink. Text label notes 'match to photo (orig. was 3.5x2)' visible near lichen specimen.

narrativeText: Most protists are too small to see, but they're far from insignificant. Protists are fundamental to healthy ecosystems. Algal protists photosynthesize to provide food for their fungal partners. All lichens are symbioses. Soil protists consume dead organic material, which contributes to the detrital food web. Algal protists provide food and habitat for snails, fish, and other small animals.
legibility: good — all specimen labels legible in crops
notes: Exhibit panel showing protists' ecological roles. Reindeer lichen (Cladonia rangiferina, August 5, 2010, Burke Mountain, BC, No. E.0337, tray 4.5" x 4.5"). narrativeText: Viruses are the most abundant and among the most diverse organisms on the planet. Fortunately, viruses are picky — each targets certain species or cells. Viruses come in different shapes and sizes, but all are tiny: ten times smaller than most bacteria. The virus that causes myxomatosis has been used in Australia to control populations of introduced rabbits.
legibility: good — all labels legible in crops
notes: Exhibit panel on virus diversity. Large cross-section diagram of a virus labeled with 'Surface proteins for host recognition' and 'Nucelic acid genome'. Magnified inset credited to Danielle Winget. Caption: 'A virus infects a marine algae'. Microscopy images: Epifluorescence image of viruses and bacteria (credit: Jerome Payet), Podophage, Cyanomyovirus, Myophage, Podovirus. European rabbit skull (Oryctolagus cuniculus, No. 4071). Photograph of Curtis Suttle's lab (University of British Columbia, 2010) — part of UBC's Departments of Earth and Ocean Sciences, Botany, and Microbiology and Immunology; trawls the ocean, plumbs caverns' depths, and scales mountains searching for viral diversity new to science.

narrativeText: Viruses need host cells to replicate. Information in their DNA or RNA is essentially locked up until they enter a living cell. Once inside, viruses steal the cell's resources to produce tens to thousands of copies of themselves. Many can cause the host cell to burst and release the new viruses. Some combine their genetic material with that of the cells and remain dormant. The Lucke tumour herpesvirus infects the kidneys of frogs like this bullfrog.
legibility: good — all text and diagram labels clear
notes: Exhibit panel on viral replication cycle. Bullfrog skull (Rana catesbeiana, Teaching collection) displayed atop anatomical illustration of frog with kidneys highlighted in red. Circular lifecycle diagram: Virus attaches to host cell -> Virus enters host cell -> Virus integrates into host genome -> Cellular replication (branch) -> Viral components made -> Components assembled into complete viruses -> Host cell bursts and releases new viruses.

narrativeText: We know viruses because of the diseases they cause. But they can also help us, such as by controlling insect pests on crops. Viruses used this way, as biocontrol agents, must be host-specific: infecting only the pest, so the viruses don't harm other organisms in the ecosystem. A viral biocontrol agent is widely used to protect Douglas fir trees in the forestry industry. The virus replicates inside the cell nuclei of the host, the caterpillar pest, killing it before it can infect another tree.
legibility: good — all labels legible in crops
notes: Exhibit panel on viral biocontrol. Line-drawing illustration of hand-held sprayer applying biocontrol agent, with red dots representing virus particles dispersing onto Douglas-fir branches. Douglas-fir branch (Pseudotsuga menziesii, January 18, 2011, UBC campus, Vancouver, BC, No. E.0313). Magnified circular inset shows caterpillar on branch (credit: USDA Forest Service). Second circular inset shows diagram of virus replicating inside cell nuclei.

narrativeText: "There is grandeur in this view of life, with its several powers, having been originally breathed into a few forms or into one; and that, whilst this planet has gone cycling on according to the fixed law of gravity, from so simple a beginning endless forms most beautiful and most wonderful have been, and are being, evolved." —Charles Darwin, On the Origin of Species, 1859
notes: Introductory panel for DI.08. Three microscope images of microorganisms on right side. Small labels beneath each image are partially legible. Protist label reads 'Protist / Pelomyxa sp.' but very small. Middle image label reads 'Legionella pneumophila'. Bottom image labeled 'H5N1 influenza virus'. No sharp crop verification was possible due to Bash permission denial.

narrativeText: Whales’ bodies show evidence of their evolutionary past. They have hipbones, even though they no longer have hind legs. These tiny bones appear to be useless to the whale, but its land-dwelling ancestors needed them, and they haven’t yet fully disappeared. Human bodies, like those of all animals, also show traces of what our ancestors looked like in vestigial features like our wisdom teeth.
notes: Blue whale pelvic bone (second specimen) labeled: Balaenoptera musculus / 1987 / Tignish, PEI / No. 17999. Whale skeleton illustration at top left credited 'Derek Tan' (small text, partially legible). Raccoon specimen labeled 'Teaching collection'. D044 visible on right edge.

narrativeText: These sea blush plants can tell us about deer on the islands they came from. Browsing by deer encourages short, bushy plants that flower in late spring – when many other tasty species are available. On islands without deer, sea blush flowers earlier, grows taller, and has fewer branches.
notes: Pressed sea blush plant specimens on left. Two seed images labeled 'Winged seeds from tall sea blush' and 'Unwinged seeds from short sea blush'. Secondary text block: 'Sea blush on islands without deer produces winged seeds. When deer are present, it produces unwinged seeds, which probably survive better in the animals' guts.' Photo at bottom left with caption referencing Peter Arcese as a professor in UBC's Department of Forest Sciences and co-director of the Centre for Applied Conservation Research, studying genetics of island organisms. Some small text partially illegible without crop zoom.

narrativeText: Durophagy is the ability to eat hard-shelled animals such as crabs and corals. Interestingly, durophagy has evolved several times in some groups of cartilaginous fishes. Although sharks and rays have relatively soft jaws made of cartilage, some are able to crush hard shellfish with highly specialized tooth plates that look more like paving stones than teeth. The plates are continuously replaced as they wear down.
notes: Five jaw specimens displayed. All five labeled 'To be accessioned' with no catalog numbers. Bowmouth guitar fish from Australia. Zebra bullhead shark from Japan. Southern stingray from Bahamas. Bottom text: 'Part of the jaw collection donated by Aidan and Anne Martin, North Vancouver, BC.' D046 visible on right edge.

narrativeText: You’re related to this harbour porpoise, but have you ever thought about what our common ancestor looked like? Eomaia scansoria was a tiny, shrew-like animal that lived about 125 million years ago. An animal that probably looked a lot like Eomaia diversified into every placental mammal alive today—including humans, porpoises, and even your pet dog!
notes: Harbour porpoise lower jaw specimen labeled: Phocoena phocoena / July 18, 1948 / Bella Bella, BC / No. 2798. Phylogenetic tree diagram shows branching from Past to Present with groups: Elephants and armadillos, Rodents, Primates, Bats, Whales and porpoises, Cattle deer pigs and hippos, Horses and rhinoceroses, Dogs cats and bears. Illustration of Eomaia scansoria (shrew-like ancestor) at top left, credited 'Derek Tan' (small). D047 visible on right edge.

narrativeText: Humans, like all vertebrates, share a relatively recent ancestor with echinoderms, a group that includes sea stars, sea urchins, and sea cucumbers. The two lineages separated about 600 million years ago. We look very different today, but echinoderms and chordates share many features, such as the way our guts, skeletons, and body cavities develop.
notes: Sea biscuit labeled: Clypeaster rosaceus / No. MI4028. Sea star labeled: Echinoidea / No. MI4163 (right side). Acorn worm illustration in center with caption: 'Our most recent common ancestor with echinoderms looked similar to this acorn worm.' Phylogenetic tree shows: Last common ancestor of echinoderms and chordates branching to Echinoderms (Sea stars, sea urchins and sea cucumbers), Hemichordates (Acorn worms and pterobranchs), and Chordates (Vertebrates and tunicates). Small illustration label partially legible. D048 visible on right edge.

narrativeText: Even humans and mushrooms are related if you look back a billion years or more on the tree of life. We’re both descended from a single-celled organism that had a flagellum (like the tail of a sperm).
notes: Fly amanita specimen labeled: Amanita muscaria / No. E.0177. Bolete specimens labeled: Boletus zelleri / No. E.0178 a-d. Images showing Human sperm and Choanoflagellates with photo credit 'images by Mark J. Bhatt' (partially legible). Phylogenetic tree from Past to Present showing: Animals, Choanoflagellates, Fungi. D049 visible on left side.

narrativeText: Want to discover a new species? Biologists have travelled the world to do just that, but you don't need to. Choose a patch of ground in a local park and scoop up some soil in a cup. Millions upon millions of microbes live in a soil sample, from bacteria to viruses to fungi to protists — most of which have never been identified. We have discovered only a fraction of Earth's species. Biodiversity hot spots — areas likely to support myriad life forms — are where many scientists look for unclassified species. A magnifying glass can help you discover new species of fungi, roundworms, arthropods in your neighbourhood. Some members of the tree of life are now found only in fossils.
taxonomyTree: Hexapoda, Crustacea, Myriapoda, Chelicerata, Trilobita (extinct), Solenogastres, Caudofoveata, Polyplacophora, Gastropoda, Bivalvia, Cephalopoda: Nautiloidea, Cephalopoda: Coleoidea, Cephalopoda: Ammonoidea (extinct)

Also includes Trilobita fossils shown on taxonomy tree.

narrativeText: Foxgloves may increase their chances of reproducing by producing different colours of flowers to attract a range of pollinators, like bees. White, light magenta, and dark magenta flowers are often found in the same area. Having more than one form, or morph, is called polymorphism. Native to Europe, the foxglove escaped gardens to become a common species in British Columbia. Among staph infections in British Columbia, resistance to methicillin rose from 3 per cent in 1999 to 24 per cent in 2009. This tiger beetle lives all over western North America. Some populations are becoming separated by mountains and other barriers. But since they still interbreed where the populations overlap, scientists don't know yet if they will become new species.
notes: Complex multi-panel composite display. Top portion shows three foxglove plants demonstrating polymorphism. Contains antibiotic resistance timeline chart showing drugs including Penicillin, Chloramphenicol, Erythromycin, Methicillin, Cephalosporin (first generation), Vancomycin, Cephalosporin (second generation), Carbapenems, Linezolid. Beetle speciation diagrams showing 'Daughter species A' and 'Daughter species B' in circles. Staphylococcus aureus (irradiated) specimen from UBC Department of Microbiology and Immunology. Placeholder circle labeled 'Placeholder Final Size TBD'. Includes hair sample panel and Oregon tiger beetle text. Drawer ID D007 visible. Some text rotated 90 degrees making reading difficult.

narrativeText: The discovery of common periwinkles in Stanley Park was an important find, because these large marine snails could alter British Columbia's shorelines. They crowd out native snails and devour algae, undermining the foundation of intertidal ecosystems. The common periwinkle has a smooth, onion-shaped shell. At up to 2.5 centimetres in diameter, it is larger than any native periwinkle. Chris Harley is an associate professor in UBC's Department of Zoology. He and his students at UBC discovered common periwinkles in Stanley Park in 2010.
notes: Display about invasive vs native periwinkles in Stanley Park, Vancouver. Common periwinkle labeled as 'Invasive'; Checkered periwinkle, Sitka periwinkle, and Raven periwinkle labeled as 'Native'. Includes map of Stanley Park and Vancouver area showing distribution. Photo of Chris Harley (University of British Columbia, 2010). Shell specimens displayed in circular insets on dark backgrounds. Drawer ID D031.

narrativeText: On taking it out of my net and opening the glorious wings, my heart began to beat violently, the blood rushed to my head, and I felt much more like fainting than I have done when in apprehension of immediate death. — Alfred Russel Wallace, on capturing a birdwing butterfly, 1869
notes: Display shows 4 birdwing butterfly specimens on dark background with Alfred Russel Wallace quote. No species labels or catalog numbers visible for individual specimens. Drawer ID D053 visible. Watermark 'TEMP' overlaid on each specimen.

narrativeText: Owls are carnivores (meat-eaters) at the top of the food chain. By dissecting their pellets, we can learn what they are eating. Large owls can kill mammals like rats, mice, and shrews, while smaller owls take animals like insects. Check out the entomology section to learn more about pollination.

narrativeText: Conifers use wind or animals to disperse their seeds. Winged seeds are carried easily in a breeze. Larger seeds are food for animals, like squirrels and birds, which may hide uneaten seeds far from the parent tree. If forgotten, seeds germinate and grow. The life-support systems hidden in the abdomen allow an insect to breathe, digest, and reproduce. All insects have six legs. Notice how all three pairs of legs extend from the thorax. Every insect has two antennae to smell, touch, taste, and communicate, and to sense sound, heat, and motion. Can you identify the body parts on these insects?

narrativeText: This is a composite overview image showing multiple exhibit display panels. Visible panels include topics on antibiotic resistance, beetles/speciation, crabs, albino birds, and others. Rotated vertical text partially legible: "...ollected in Vancouver. The samples..." discussing hair inheritance. Tiktaalik fossils referenced: "Tiktaalik were first discovered in 2004 on Ellesmere Island, Nunavut." Photo credit: Collin Purrington.

narrativeText: Parents have offspring that look like them. So when two pea plants that produce wrinkled peas mate, their offspring should make wrinkled peas, too — or so you might think. To understand how they could have some offspring with smooth peas, you'd have to look at the grandparents: open the next drawer to find out. "Father of modern genetics" Gregor Mendel researched heredity in the 19th century by studying thousands of pea plants. The common periwinkle has a smooth, onion-shaped shell. At up to 2.5 centimetres in diameter, it is larger than any native periwinkle. Chris Harley is an associate professor in UBC's Department of Zoology. He and his students at UBC discovered common periwinkles in Stanley Park in 2010.
notes: Display panel combining Mendelian genetics (pea plants) with invasive periwinkle species in Stanley Park/Vancouver. Map of Stanley Park and Vancouver visible. Small shell specimens displayed in circular insets. Pea plant herbarium specimen catalog number not legible in crops. University of British Columbia, 2010 credited for periwinkle research.

narrativeText: Genes from two parents don't blend together in the parents' offspring, but remain separate: it's like trying to mix two colours of jellybeans. Some effects are visible in the offsprings' appearance, but others remain hidden, to be revealed in future generations — like when two brown-eyed parents have a blue-eyed child. The genes from the grandparents that produced smooth peas were hidden in the parents, but revealed in the offspring. "Sea squirt" may be a cute name, but don't be fooled by these invasive animals. These tunicates should be arrested for stealing vital space and food from native ones. Free from their usual predators, invasive sea squirts grow and multiply quickly — suffocating other marine life. Cedar boughs, laden with herring roe (foreground), and racks of herring were set out to dry near Scuttle Bay in Sliammon traditional territory. This photo, identified by Sliammon elder Mary George as her parents' backyard in 1939, shows a community practice that had continued for at least 900 years. Herring were once abundant along the BC coast, forming a cornerstone of human and ecological communities. But their populations have collapsed from commercial overfishing. Scientists are now turning to First Nations' traditional ecological knowledge and archaeological evidence to help determine past population numbers, so that we can better understand how to bring herring back. Dana Lepofsky is a professor in Simon Fraser University's Department of Archaeology. She coordinates the "Herring School," a collaboration between scientists and people from First Nations communities working on herring research and conservation.
notes: Display panel combining Mendelian genetics continuation (pea plant cross diagram showing grandparents/parents/offspring with smooth and wrinkled peas), invasive sea squirts, and herring conservation on BC coast. References drawer D032 and D186. Photo credit: Powell River Museum. Dana Lepofsky, Simon Fraser University, 2011. No specimen labels with catalog numbers visible.

narrativeText: Which one of these baby mice do you think is more likely to belong to this mother mouse? Hint: babies tend to look like their parents. When parents have offspring that look like themselves, we call it inheritance. What features do you share with your parents? Are all introduced species bad? Not always. Manila, varnish, and softshell clams have spread without noticeably harming British Columbia's coastal ecosystems. Accidentally introduced through the oyster trade, they are now harvested commercially and added to chowders, soups, and appetizers — to the delight of many seafood connoisseurs. Notice the distinctive large hinge at the end of the softshell clam's shell. Basket sedges are disappearing, and so is the knowledge of gathering and weaving them. Traditional collecting areas are being lost as the timber industry changes water flows, and elders are finding fewer young people with time to learn what they have to teach. Pakki Chipps-Sawyer has a PhD from the University of Victoria in ethnobotany and education, and is a status member of the Beecher Bay First Nation in Sooke, BC. She has written about the importance of traditional languages and plant knowledge.
notes: Display panel combining inheritance (house mice specimens), introduced clam species, and ethnobotany (basket sedges). Slough sedge specimen: 2010, Jordan River, BC. References drawer D033 and D189. Pakki Chipps-Sawyer, University of Victoria, 2011. Softshell clam specimen shown in petri dish, catalog number not visible.

narrativeText: Birds do it, bees do it...in fact, researchers estimate that over 99.9 per cent of eukaryotes have sex. It turns out that sexual reproduction is older than scientists used to think, emerging over a billion years ago — not long after our branch of the tree of life split off from bacteria. Eukaryotes include all organisms whose cells contain a nucleus (from tapeworms to mushrooms and from oak trees to humans). Bacteria share genes too, but not by mating. Stone tools tell t... At one time worth its weight in gold, pepper was traded across thousands of miles through Asia, Africa, and Europe. When the fall of Constantinople (modern-day Istanbul) disrupted these routes in 1453, the race to find a new spice route led to the European discovery of the Americas, and attempts to grow spices elsewhere in the tropics.
notes: Display panel combining sexual reproduction (snowshoe hare specimens, tree of life diagram showing Eubacteria/Archaebacteria/Eukaryotes, rabbit silhouette series), stone tools, and spice trade history. Snowshoe hares: June 18, 1947, Huntington, BC. Map shows old spice routes (15th century) and new spice routes (16th century onwards) with Constantinople marked. Black pepper peppercorn strands displayed. References drawer D191.

narrativeText: From one cell... to a cluster of cells... to simple sponges... to more complex structures. Humans are made up of 100 trillion cells, but most organisms on Earth consist of only one cell. Over time, some multicellular organisms evolved several times independently from different single-celled ancestors. Sponges were some of the earliest multicellular animals. Success is not always about going it alone; sometimes joining forces is the best way to survive. Strong aromas like pine, citrus, and mint all come from one group of plant chemicals, called terpenes, often found in tree bark. Many of these chemicals have been shown to ward off insects. Whatever its use to the plant, the terpene taxol, from the bark of Pacific yew, helps fight cancer. Taxol is now synthesized in the lab, sparing wild populations of Pacific yew.
notes: Display panel combining multicellularity evolution (choanoflagellate cell illustration, Sphaeroeca colony photo, sponges), terpene chemistry (yew specimens), and abalone conservation. Taxus sp.: August 8, 2011, UBC campus, Vancouver, BC. Pacific yew bark: August 7, 2011, Emerald Forest Conservation Area, Whistler, BC. Northern abalones: September 2009, Bamfield Huu-ay-aht Community Abalone Project hatchery, Vancouver Island, BC. Venus's flower basket sponge specimen dimensions noted 7.75x9in. Also visible: cleared/stained bird specimen (feet). References drawer D213.

narrativeText: Food goes in the mouth, poop goes out the anus. At least, that's how we work. Some animals have just one opening for both jobs. Anemones and corals have sac-like guts. This coral is made up of hundreds of these tiny organisms. Worms — like humans — evolved a one-way gut where food goes into the mouth and solid waste is released through the anus. Stone tools tell the story... hammers, rock picks, trowels, and screens. Back in the lab, some fossils are cleaned with water; others are further uncovered by a small sandblaster that erodes away soft rock around the fossil. Hannes Dempewolf studies the evolution and human use of noug in Ethiopia. He hopes his work on noug diversity and breeding will help improve food security in sub-Saharan Africa.
notes: Display panel combining digestive system evolution (coral specimen with polyp diagram, earthworm in jar specimen with digestive tract diagram), fossil preparation (paleontology tools displayed, fossil jaw/mandible with red museum label), and agricultural research. Earthworm: December 10, 2010, Elk Lake Park, Vancouver Island. Coral specimen displayed but species name not legible. Trilobite referenced. Hannes Dempewolf, University of British Columbia, 2011. Photo credits: Scott Black. Line drawings of butterfly and grasshopper (increasing size series) visible at bottom. References drawer D014.

narrativeText: This tiger beetle lives all over western North America. Some populations are becoming separated by mountains and other barriers. But since they still interbreed where their populations overlap, scientists don't know yet if they will become new species.
notes: Exhibit panel showing three topics: (1) antibiotic resistance timeline chart (Bars run from the year of introduction of a drug to the year in which resistance was observed; drugs include Penicillin, Chloramphenicol, Erythromycin, Methicillin, Cephalosporin, Vancomycin, Cephalosporins second generation, Carbapenems, Linezolid); (2) MRSA/staph infections section with microscope image of bacteria and text 'Among staph infections in British Columbia, resistance to methicillin rose from 3 per cent in 1999 to 24 per cent in 2009'; placeholder circle labeled 'Placeholder Final Size TBD'; (3) Oregon tiger beetle speciation section with circles showing Daughter species A and Daughter species B with displayed beetle specimens. Center panel text begins 'All this hair was collected in Vancouver.' July 17, 2010, Iona Beach Park. Labels reference 50,000x magnification.

narrativeText: The common periwinkle has a smooth, onion-shaped shell. At up to 2.5 centimetres in diameter, it is larger than any native periwinkle. Plants can adapt to dry conditions, but at a cost. A plant in a wetter climate can put more energy into growth or reproduction. So in wet agricultural fields, selection favours plants that grow faster than those in drier natural areas nearby. However, if there's a drought, plants from agricultural fields get stressed faster than those adapted to drier areas.
notes: Composite exhibit panel. Top section: plant adaptation experiment showing Non-stressed vs Stressed leaves for Agricultural and Native varieties. Bottom section: invasive periwinkle species in Vancouver/Stanley Park area. Map shows Stanley Park and Vancouver area. Researcher: Chris Harley, associate professor in UBC's Department of Zoology (University of British Columbia, 2010). He and his students discovered common periwinkles in Stanley Park in 2010. Researcher: Maya Mayrent (partial text visible), post-doctoral fellow at UBC's Department of Botany, studies plant defence mechanisms. Shell specimens displayed in circular insets. Panel ID D031 visible in top-right area.

narrativeText: Are all introduced species bad? Not always. Manila, varnish, and softshell clams have spread without noticeably harming British Columbia's coastal ecosystems. Accidentally introduced through the oyster trade, they are now harvested commercially and added to chowders, soups, and appetizers -- to the delight of many seafood connoisseurs. How can this fly maze help us think about selection? When a fruit fly enters the tube, it decides whether to turn up or down. Only the flies that go up are allowed to breed. After just a few generations, the flies mainly go up. Notice the distinctive large hinge at the end of the softshell clam's shell. Basket sedges are disappearing, and so is the knowledge of gathering and weaving them. Traditional collecting areas are being lost as the timber industry changes water flows, and elders are finding fewer young people with time to learn what they have to teach.
notes: Composite exhibit panel with multiple topics. Top section: fruit fly maze selection experiment with Yes/No tube apparatus and illustration of Drosophila. Middle section: introduced clam species -- softshell clam (Mya arenaria) in petri dish and varnish clam (Nuttalia obscurata) opened to show interior; Manila clam referenced in text but not labeled as displayed. Bottom section: basket sedges topic with dried plant material shown; researcher Pakki Chipps-Sawyer (University of Victoria, 2011), PhD in ethnobotany and education, status member of the Beecher Bay First Nation in Sooke, BC, written about importance of traditional languages and plant knowledge. Panel IDs D033 and D189 visible.

narrativeText: Selection can happen fast in a garden or a farmer's field. When pesticides are sprayed, a few insects may survive to lay eggs, passing on the genes that helped them survive. So when their offspring are sprayed, a few more will survive. In just a few generations, most of the insects can become resistant. In BC greenhouses, researchers found that the cabbage looper moth quickly evolved resistance to the organic pesticide Bt.
notes: Composite exhibit panel with multiple topics. Top section: pesticide resistance in cabbage looper moths, with Before Bt use / After Bt use / A few generations later butterfly diagrams. Cabbage looper photo on flower; Thuricide (Bacillus thuringiensis) bottle shown; scanning electron microscope image of Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt). Cabbage loopers (larvae, pupae, and adult) specimens displayed, December 8, 2010, Beaty Biodiversity Centre (lab), Vancouver, BC. Middle section: Golden star tunicate (colonial, invasive) Botryllus schlosseri with 100x magnification image; Violet tunicate Botrylloides violaceus (invasive, on mussel). Invasive sea squirts on what appears to be aquaculture equipment. Bottom section: herring/archaeology topic about overfishing and First Nations traditional knowledge, researcher Dana Lepofsky (Simon Fraser University, 2011), professor in Department of Archaeology; text about herring becoming a cornerstone, populations collapsed from overfishing, turning to First Nations traditional ecological knowledge and archaeological evidence to determine past population numbers and how to bring herring back. Panel ID D186 visible.

narrativeText: Geographic barriers, like mountains and rivers, can cause new species to evolve. In Asia, greenish warblers spread out in two directions around the Tibetan plateau. When they met again on the other side, after a very long time, the birds on the two ends couldn't interbreed, even though they can mate with their neighbours all the way around the ring. Biologists call this situation a "ring species." At one time worth its weight in gold, pepper was traded across thousands of miles through Asia, Africa, and Europe. When the fall of Constantinople (modern-day Istanbul) disrupted these routes in 1453, the race to find a new spice route led to the European discovery of the Americas, and attempts to grow spices elsewhere in the tropics.
notes: Composite exhibit panel. Top section: greenish warbler ring species around Tibetan Plateau with range map showing Russia, India, Northern contact zone. Wing bar width chart showing variation across populations. Feather specimens displayed (8' x 3' display case). Researcher: Darren Irwin (University of British Columbia, 2010), associate professor in UBC's Department of Zoology, studies speciation. Bottom section: spice trade routes map showing Old spice routes (15th century, in red through Constantinople) and New spice routes (16th century onwards, in blue around Africa). Black pepper plant specimens displayed. Text about Stone tools visible at edge. Panel ID D191 visible.

narrativeText: Many plants have more than two sets of chromosomes, making them polyploid. Chromosomes carry the genetic information that determines all of an organism's features, including visible traits like colour and height. The combination of more than two sets of chromosomes can create new variations -- and sometimes, new species. In the early 1900s, three species of European Tragopogon, including this one, were introduced to North America. Today, through hybridization and polyploidy, two new species have emerged.
notes: Composite exhibit panel. Top section: UBC Herbarium pressed specimen of Tragopogon dubius (Yellow salsify), collected June 13, 1972, at Junction of Chilcotin and Fraser Rivers, BC. Herbarium sheet includes color calibration card and 'Imaged' stamp. Middle section: poster titled 'Major Insect Orders of British Columbia' with detailed scientific illustrations by Derek Tan of orders including Collembola, Microcoryphia, Zygentoma, Ephemeroptera, Odonata, Grylloptera, Grylloblattodea, Plecoptera, Dictyoptera, Orthoptera, Isoptera, Phthiraptera, Psocoptera, Hymenoptera, Thysanoptera, Hemiptera, Neuroptera, Megaloptera, Mecoptera, and others. Bottom section: archival glue bottle and plant specimens (moss/bryophyte and leafy branch with berries visible).

narrativeText: These little brown wrens, which look almost identical but sing very different songs, are actually two different species. Evidence suggests that they split apart over four million years ago, when one population was separated into two different areas. But both occur in Tumbler Ridge, BC, and UBC research in this contact zone has shown that they don't interbreed. The Pacific Wren was officially recognized as a species in July 2010, as a result of UBC research. Although this parrot isn't endangered, its trade is regulated by international law, like that of most parrots. Parrots face a dual threat: habitat loss and the pet trade have combined to endanger 94 of the world's parrot species. Thousands of animals are still taken from sensitive habitats every year, but captive breeding programs have helped to relieve the pressure in some places.
notes: Composite exhibit panel. Top section: Pacific wren and Winter wren speciation with range map of North America showing Pacific wren range (blue, western) and Winter wren range (red/dashed, eastern), contact zone at Tumbler Ridge. Two study skin specimens displayed (6.5' x 5' case). Researcher: David Toews (University of British Columbia, 2010), PhD student in Darren Irwin's research group in UBC Department of Zoology, studies how new species of birds form and adapt to different environments. Bottom section: White cockatoo (Cacatua alba) study skin specimen (~6x18in), July 2010, World Parrot Refuge. Foot tag visible with catalog number 16204 and 'Cacatua alba'. Panel ID D149 visible.

narrativeText: All this hair was collected in Vancouver. The samples show the diversity of our population, but also how the hair from children from the same family looks more like each other's than from other families. Physical features are inherited, because a child's traits are inherited from its parents. Blah blah blah blah blah. This is about science—sort of—this is about the maximum possible size of text we should ever, ever have. Hopefully, I think. Surely, no longer than this line here.
taxonomyTree: Archaeoceti (Ancient whales, extinct) > Mysticeti (Baleen whales) + Odontoceti (Toothed whales)

The display appears to combine whale specimens with an unrelated hair diversity panel.

narrativeText: The ancient ancestors of whales were hard of hearing, at least compared with their modern descendants, like our blue whale. They evolved from land-dwelling animals, so their ears weren't much use underwater. So over the course of tens of millions of years, whales evolved a new system of hearing, gradually losing the old system as they honed the new. Fossil evidence has allowed scientists to reconstruct the transition in detail. Bones from archaeological sites can help us understand the former abundance of herring.
notes: Multi-panel composite image. Top section: whale ear ossicles (fossil from Hawthorne Formation, Miocene, South Carolina; and modern blue whale from 1987, Tignish, Prince Edward Island) with whale skeleton diagram. Middle/bottom section: intertidal zone display with three zones labeled (high tide zone with 'dryness and extreme temperatures', middle tide zone with 'regular submersion and exposure', low tide zone with 'brief periods of dryness'). narrativeText: Every flower is made of some combination of the same four basic parts. Leaves that helped perform certain functions benefited the plant, and over millions of years they evolved into new structures: sepals to protect the growing bud, petals to attract pollinators, stamens to release pollen, and carpels to protect the eggs. In plants in the lily family, petals and sepals look the same.
notes: Display about plant reproduction. Shows flower anatomy diagram (stamen, carpels, petal, sepal) and two life cycle diagrams: a conifer (pine) cycle with pollen cones, seed cones, fertilization, seedling, mature plant stages; and a flowering plant (garden pea) cycle with flowers, fertilization (stigma, pollen, stamen, ovary, egg, ovule), fruit, seeds, seedling, mature plant stages. Douglas fir cones labeled: April 11, 2011, Vancouver, BC. Garden pea labeled: July 30, 2011, Point Roberts, Washington. Pine labeled bottom-right with partial number visible. Display panel code D057 visible on adjacent panel.

narrativeText: If you want to see an albino bird, start with crows and robins. They're more often seen than most other birds. True albinos completely lack a pigment called melanin, and are pure white with pink eyes. Animals that are leucistic have reduced levels of all types of skin pigments, not just melanin. They're paler than normal, but usually have dark eyes.
notes: Display shows three plumage variants of Northwestern crow: normal black, albino, and leucistic. Black specimen collected March 4, 1994, Maple Ridge, BC. Albino specimen collected June 11, 1992, Surrey, BC. Leucistic specimen collected September 24, 1939, Port Coquitlam, BC. Display panel code D074 visible.

narrativeText: There are five different species of cowries here. What is a species anyway? In biology, a species is often defined as a group of organisms, or genetic community, that interbreed and produce fertile offspring. Scientists use similarities and differences among organisms as clues to decide if they are different species. At one time worth its weight in gold, pepper was traded across thousands of miles through Asia, Africa, and Europe. When the fall of Constantinople (modern-day Istanbul) disrupted these routes in 1453, the race to find a new spice route led to the European discovery of the Americas, and attempts to grow spices elsewhere in the tropics. Stone tools tell t...

Bottom section: spice trade history with world map showing old spice routes (15th century, in red through Constantinople) and new spice routes (16th century onwards, in blue around Africa and to the Americas). Black pepper specimen displayed with peppercorns. Display panel code D191 visible.

narrativeText: East meets west when it comes to yellow-rumped warblers. After the ice age, two species began interbreeding in a contact zone in the Rocky Mountains. But researchers have found that even after hybridizing for thousands of years, the birds have still kept their distinct colour patterns.
notes: Display shows four yellow-rumped warbler specimens (male and female of both D. auduboni auduboni and D. coronata hooveri subspecies) with a map of North America showing D. auduboni breeding zone (yellow/green, western) and D. coronata breeding zone (orange/brown, eastern) with a contact zone in the Rocky Mountains. Female D. a. auduboni collected May 4, 1929, Pitt Meadows, BC (during migration). Male D. a. auduboni collected May 12, 1928, Pitt Meadows, BC (during migration). Female D. c. hooveri collected May 12, 1929, North Vancouver, BC (during migration). Male D. c. hooveri collected May 4, 1929, Pitt Meadows, BC (during migration). narrativeText: It's not always easy to tell species apart just by looking at them. These mushrooms, for example, both look like tasty wild chanterelles. But they're actually very different species — one is good to eat, but the other gives some people stomachaches. The small pools of water that collect inside bromeliads can host complex webs of interactions among organisms. "In nature's economy the currency is not money, it is life." — Vandana Shiva, Earth Democracy, 2005. ...Vancouver is the smell of skunk. They're carnivores, and normally eat ...eggs, but in the city, they're just as likely to go after your garbage. ...Vancouver are all descended ...ey Park a...
