(October 1, 1997), Scirtidae (September 2003, E.0144), Culex jenningsi (October 1997). Mecistogaster modesta larva shown as top-level predator in vial. Bromeliad plant displayed as 'Actual...' (label cut off). Bottom sections: Douglas squirrel (native) and striped skunk (August 1977, Abbotsford, BC) with narrative about urban wildlife in Vancouver. Historical painting by Rita Greer also visible. Display panel code D148 visible.

narrativeText: "The affinities of all the beings of the same class have sometimes been represented by a great tree... As buds give rise by growth to fresh buds, and these if vigorous, branch out and overtop on all sides many a feebler branch, so by generation I believe it has been with the great Tree of Life, which fills with its dead and broken branches the crust of the earth, and covers the surface with its ever branching and beautiful ramifications." —Charles Darwin, On the Origin of Species, 1859
notes: Display panel illustrating Tree of Life concept. Metallic wood-boring beetle label states family Buprestidae, 1966-67, Chiang Mai, Thailand. Yellow-crowned Amazon from World Parrot Refuge, Coombs, BC. Musk mallow collected July 2010 at intersection of SW Marine Drive and 16th Avenue, Vancouver, BC. Southern baler from Western Australia. Phylogenetic tree diagram in center. Background features Darwin's divergence diagram. Small text at bottom edge reads 'Just four crops—rice...' and 'three-quarters of the...' (partially cut off, belonging to adjacent panel).

narrativeText: Imagine if you and your friend were working on solving the same problem. You might come up with the same solution, even if you worked separately. Other living things do this too. Look at desert plants. Just like American cacti, this Old World plant has evolved a fleshy, succulent stem that stores water and helps it survive in the dry heat of the desert. This feature evolved separately—scientists say convergently—in both groups.
notes: Complex multi-panel display. Euphorbia stygiana label: August 16, 2011, UBC Botanical Garden Nurseries BC. Saguaro cactus shown as illustration (referenced). Oregon spotted frog labeled as endangered, from Abbotsford, BC. Tent caterpillars and moths from Southern Gulf Islands, BC. Tent caterpillar webs from Saturna Island, Lower Mainland, BC. Panel includes map of Lower Mainland showing population density, a graph of tent caterpillar populations over several decades (1980-2010), and text about UBC researchers studying tent caterpillar populations in British Columbia. Also includes text about Oregon spotted frog being Canada's most endangered amphibian, fewer than 300 remain. Triangular prism panel with text about hair/DNA. Additional text about specimens collected in Vancouver.

narrativeText: These animals have evolved separately for hundreds of millions of years, in different environments. So why do they all have such similar long, narrow jaws and sharp, cone-shaped teeth? They all eat fish, so had to adapt to holding onto wriggling, slippery prey. The three lineages converged on unique but similar solutions to the same problem. Dolphins' and caimans' teeth aren't attached to the jaw like a pike's. Like yours, they sit in sockets.
notes: Caiman skull from Teaching collection (no catalog number). Striped dolphin jaw dated April 22, 1961, from Long Point Provincial Park, BC. Number 8011 written on specimen. Pike jaw specimen is a bone preparation.

narrativeText: Flight is a good idea reinvented. Wings in vertebrates evolved in three separate groups: pterosaurs (winged reptiles), birds, and bats. The fact that it evolved so many times shows how useful it is. In how many ways can you imagine flight being useful?
notes: Panel features line drawings of a pterosaur and a bat alongside a real Common poorwill wing specimen. Pterosaur illustration credited to David Gilbor. Panel code D081.

narrativeText: Why do whales have their noses in the air? Cetaceans—marine mammals like whales, dolphins, and porpoises—evolved from land mammals. When they first moved back to the water, they had to hold their snouts above the surface to breathe. Animals with nostrils closer to the tops of their heads had an advantage, and over millions of years their nostrils evolved into the blowhole.
notes: Harbour porpoise skull displayed, viewed from top showing blowhole position. Handwritten specimen tag reads: Cowan Collection, No. 17159, Name: Harbour Porpoise, Phocoena phocoena, West Coast Vancouver Island, BC, Date: 1996, Col: Kelly Heiss, Colin Sanders. Printed label reads: Harbour porpoise, Phocoena phocoena, 1996, West Coast Vancouver Island, BC, No. 17159. Blue whale illustration by Artiom Shostak. Panel code D082.

narrativeText: Hundreds of populations of stickleback fish were stranded in BC coastal lakes after the last ice age. In just 10,000 years, they have adapted in the same ways to similar environments in different lakes. Their predictable, yet independently evolved adaptations to the same kinds of environments confirms that natural selection is driving their evolution.
notes: Two-panel image. Top panel: Stickleback parallel evolution. Four wet specimens in jars from two lakes: Little Quarry Lake, Nelson Island, BC (Summer 2007) and Paxton Lake, Texada Island, BC (2009). Diagram shows limnetic zone vs benthic zone in a lake cross-section. Photo and bio of Dolph Schluter, University of British Columbia, 2010 — professor and Canada Research Chair in UBC's Department of Zoology who studies rapid parallel evolution of BC sticklebacks. Panel code D083. Bottom panel: Fossil excavation. Trilobite fossil (pyrite replacement), Cambrian, Antelope Springs, Utah, No. 2008-405. Tools displayed: ruler, trowel, brush, chisel kit. Photo of fossil excavation at Drummond, Montana (credit: Craig Christensen). Narrative: Unearthing fossils is a bit like mining with small miner's tools — hammers, rock picks, trowels, and screens. Back in the lab, some fossils are cleaned with water; others are further uncovered by a small sandblaster that erodes away soft rock around the fossil. Panel code D014.

narrativeText: Human hands, bat wings, bird wings, horse hooves, dolphin flippers, and mole paws all do different things, but all are based on the same pattern of bones. This is because all these animals descended from a common ancestor. Can you see how your own hand is like this flipper and wing?
notes: Two-panel image. Top panel: Pentadactyl (five-fingered) limb homology. Displays bird wing bone and harbour porpoise flipper bone specimens alongside illustrations of bird (flying), bat (flying), horse (running), mole (digging), and dolphin (swimming) limb bone structures. Bird wing from Teaching collection. Panel code D084. Bottom panel: Noug (Guizotia abyssinica) oil-seed crop from Ethiopia. Petri dish with noug specimen labeled by hand. Narrative: Most people know little about noug, an important oil-seed crop in Ethiopia, where it grows in places so wet that many other crops can't survive. Researchers are studying noug diversity and working with Ethiopian farmers and scientists to breed varieties of noug that produce more and are easier to farm. In Canada, noug seeds are known as 'nyier' or 'thistle seeds' and sold as food for finches. Photo and bio of Hannes Dempewolf, University of British Columbia, 2011, who studies evolution and human use of noug in Ethiopia. Photo credit: Scott Black. Panel code D110. Landscape photo of noug field in Ethiopia.

narrativeText: Bogs have layers. The top layer is living Sphagnum moss, which grows upward as its lower portions die and become the partially decayed, sponge-like peat moss below. The lower layers are acidic and low in oxygen, so the shoots rot slowly. Although this peat moss is "dead," it can persist for hundreds of years.
notes: Full image shows bog/peat display with core sample (Pacific Spirit Park, BC, No. E.0177), living Sphagnum moss label, Sphagnum peat label, and Camosun Bog photo (Pacific Spirit Park, Vancouver, BC). Crops also show: Nautiloid (Actinoceras sp., Ordovician, Dawson Geology Club collection); Crinoid (Isocrinus nehalemensis, Keasey Formation, Oligocene, Mist, Oregon, No. 2008-128a-470); quote 'O earth, what changes hast thou seen!' (Tennyson, 'In Memoriam A.H.H.,' 1850); bone specimens with bird wing anatomical diagram and text about homologous structures in wings, flippers, mole paws; display reference numbers D176 and D084 visible.

narrativeText: These plants are bog specialists. Most plants can't tolerate the harsh conditions of the bog, but a select number thrive here. Bog water is acidic, so the plants have evolved to soak it up slowly. Many bog plants are in the heath family, Ericaceae.
notes: Full image shows bog plants display with Labrador tea and Bog laurel specimens, bog photo, and narrative about bog specialists in Ericaceae. Crops show insect metamorphosis panel: 'Complete metamorphosis' — about 88 per cent of insects grow this way, larvae are eating machines, pupae are transformers, adults designed for reproduction; 'Incomplete metamorphosis' — about 12 per cent of insects grow this way, nymphs shed outer skins many times before reaching adulthood. Silkworm (Bombyx mori) life cycle specimen block: 1) Egg, 2) Larva, 3) Pupa, 4) Adult. Grasshopper (Orthoptera) life cycle specimen block: 1) Egg, 2) Nymph, 3) Adult. Line drawings of butterfly and grasshopper. Display reference D157 visible.

commonGroupName: Sphagnum Mosses and Crop Seed Diversity
narrativeText: Most plants need the right environment to grow. But Sphagnum changes its environment to suit itself. It takes hold in a damp, mildly acidic environment, then produces more acid, making the bog increasingly inhospitable for other plants and more favourable for itself. | Crop diversity protects our food supply by providing insurance against diseases, pests, changing climates, and other threats. But as agriculture globalizes, worldwide crop diversity is shrinking fast. To help preserve this diversity for future generations, thousands of varieties of seeds are stored in seed banks around the world.

Legend: Non-Sphagnum peat, Decaying organic matter, Living Sphagnum, Decaying Sphagnum (peat). Richards bio (Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada, 2011) — research manager for Canadian Genetic Resource Program. Multiple seed varieties displayed in small cups. Display reference D111 visible. narrativeText: Life's not always easy for mosses in the canopy. In summer, they have to be able to withstand both drying out and the leaves of their host tree blocking the sun. But in autumn, the leaves fall and the rains come, bringing sunlight and water so the moss can thrive again. | Thank a pollinator for every third bite of food you eat. Pollination by insects, birds, and bats is responsible for about 30 per cent of the world's food supply. When crops are exposed to a variety and abundance of pollinators, food production soars. More pollinators equals more berries, fruits, vegetables, nuts, and even chocolate to satisfy your palate.

Asteraceae), Coffee beans (Coffea arabica, F. Rubiaceae), Mustard seeds (Brassica juncea, F. Brassicaceae), Brazil nuts (Bertholletia excelsa, F. Lecythidaceae), Allspice seeds (Pimenta dioica, F. Myrtaceae); also Hazelnut shells, Pecans, Almond shells, Walnut shells, Watermelon seeds (partially visible), Chocolate (Lindt Excellence 90% Cacao). Line drawing of sunflower with bee. commonGroupName: Moss Micro-organisms, Ammonites, and Edible Plants
narrativeText: You can think of moss communities as miniature forests. Under the green canopy is a protected world where small organisms such as mites, springtails, and worms are sheltered from environmental extremes. These organisms are important food for creatures higher up in the food chain. | Ammonites ruled the seas for about 200 million years, but their diversity plummeted at the end of the Triassic, when the number of genera shrank from over 100 to three. The ammonites rebounded within three million years, but full restoration of their diversity took 10 times that long. They finally became extinct at the end of the Cretaceous. | Even nettles are tasty when lightly cooked to disarm the sting!
notes: Full image shows grid of microscopic moss-dwelling organism photos (mites, springtails, etc.) with narrative about moss communities as miniature forests. Moss makes excellent building material because it is flexible and durable. | Rufous hummingbirds and chickadees are two local species that use moss to build their nests. | This humble tongue... [text continues off-frame] ...the ultimate ...coast, where ...benefit from the ...rich production ...urchins ...if there aren't enough otters to keep these kelp-eating herbivores in check. When sea urchins mow down kelp forests, scientists call what's left behind 'urchin barrens.'

narrativeText: Plants dominate the surface of the Earth. We use them... What is a plant? The answers lead us at once to a simple definition of a plant: a multicellular organism that makes its own food by photosynthesis. But this is misleading, because it is a description that includes groups of organisms that evolved photosynthesis independently. In fact, all true plants evolved from a single group of green algae that colonized the land about 480 million years ago. Called "the queen of poisons," this monkshood has been a weapon of choice for assassins dating back to Medea, of Greek mythology. So powerful is aconitine, the deadly neurotoxin the plant produces, it was rumoured in the Middle Ages that unhappy wives would wash their husbands' shirts in a monkshood brew to commit undetectable murder. In plants in the lily family, petals and sepals look the same.
taxonomyTree: Phylogenetic tree on green leaf graphic showing: Liverworts, Hornworts, Mosses, Lycophytes, Ferns, Horsetails, Seed plants, Conifers, Flowering plants, Ginkgo, Cycads — all branching from Green algae ancestor

narrativeText: If we gave out awards for evolutionary ingenuity, cyanobacteria would win first prize. They figured out, nearly 2.7 billion years ago, how to convert the sun's rays into stored chemical energy (sugar), using photosynthesis. Not to be outdone, single-celled eukaryotes swallowed and enslaved some of these tiny sugar factories, which then evolved into chloroplasts — now found in all true plants. Fossil traces left by cyanobacteria are some of the earliest evidence of life on Earth. Oxygen released by the first photosynthetic bacteria caused iron in the primordial oceans to rust, leaving this distinct evidence of early life. There are five different species of cowries here. These varieties of sunflower were cultivated by Native Americans in the locations shown, and are still important today.
notes: Large panoramic image containing multiple exhibit sections: cyanobacteria/photosynthesis timeline panel, cowrie shell display, and sunflower cultivation map. Timeline spans from Mesoproterozoic Era to present. Eukaryotic host cell diagram with chloroplast shown. Sunflower varieties labeled include 'Seneca' and 'Hopi dye'. Researcher: Nolan Kane, post-doctoral fellow in UBC's Department of Botany, University of British Columbia, 2011. Origin of domestication marked on map of North America.

narrativeText: Red, green, and brown algae are all multicellular eukaryotes that have chloroplasts for photosynthesis, but are not considered true plants. All brown algae, such as kelps, and most green and red algae are aquatic, living almost exclusively in oceans and freshwater. Only the green algae managed to overcome the constraints of aquatic living to give rise to the first land plants.

narrativeText: Spores are the first fossil evidence of land plants. Green algae living in ponds ultimately adapted to life on land, evolving into groups like mosses and liverworts. To keep from drying out, they developed a waxy cuticle. They also began dispersing their reproductive spores through the air. Flowering plants entice insects to deliver their pollen to suitable plant mates. You're probably familiar with the advertising strategy. Their showy, often fragrant flowers attract insects that then collect a reward, such as nectar, found inside. It took millions of years for the insects and flowers to work out these special relationships.
notes: Large panoramic image. Main visible section shows bryophytes (mosses/liverworts) panel with cuticular wax SEM image and Polytrichum spores micrograph. Researcher: Lacey Samuels, professor in UBC Department of Botany, studies adaptations in plants to life on land such as waxy cuticles and vascular tissues. University of British Columbia, 2011. narrativeText: Roots, leaves, and a vein-like vascular system evolved around 415 million years ago. These evolutionary milestones helped early vascular plants — similar to club mosses, horsetails, and ferns — move water from roots to leaves, and sugars from leaves to soil. This helped them spread beyond the water's edge to create vast green landscapes. Insects are the most diverse group of animals in your backyard — and the easiest to meet. But how well do you know them? Look closely: how many kinds of bees can you count? Can you find a true bug that feeds on the juices of a plant? Which of these insects would you most likely find near water? Is there one you've never seen before?

narrativeText: Land plants reproduce using sperm and eggs. For 150 million years, sperm had to swim through water to reach the egg, so plants had to reproduce close to the moist ground. Pollen and seeds changed all that. Pollen carries sperm through the air, and seeds protect and nourish young embryos. Orchids, buttercups, lilies, and roses aren't just for the garden. In our local forests, plants from these and dozens more in a myriad of colours and shapes await the careful observer in spring and summer. Once pollinated and fertilized, most flowers develop fruits with seeds. Fruits come in different shapes and sizes, but each has the same job: to protect and help disperse the plant's seeds.
notes: Large panoramic image. narrativeText: Plants with flowers are called angiosperms. Attracting animals with showy flowers for reproduction and tasty fruit for seed dispersal proved a great success. Animals and flowering plants have diversified together for almost 140 million years. Flowering plants use many means to disperse their fruit and seeds, such as wind, animals, water, or explosive capsules. This helps new seedlings grow far from their parents to avoid competing for water, light, and nutrients.

narrativeText: Most, but not all, conifers have pollen and seed cones on the same tree. Conifers rely on wind for pollination. A cone is not a fruit, but a structure that produces seeds or pollen. All conifers have separate pollen and ovulate (seed) cones. An ovulate cone produces one or many eggs. Sperm from pollen grains, carried on the wind, fertilize the eggs, which then develop into seeds. Pollen cones are small and simple in structure, but seed cones can grow much larger -- up to half a metre long! Life cycle of a conifer.

narrativeText: Conifers use wind or animals to disperse their seeds. Winged seeds are carried easily in a breeze. Larger seeds are food for animals, like squirrels and birds, which may hide uneaten seeds far from the parent tree. If forgotten, seeds germinate and grow. Whitebark pine relies heavily on the Clark's nutcracker for seed dispersal. Each bird can cache up to 100,000 seeds each year. Look for the 'wings' on the seeds of Douglas-firs. They help the wind catch and disperse the seeds.

narrativeText: What do you see in this drawer: fruits or vegetables? If you said fruits, you think like a botanist—a scientist who studies plants. Fruits contain the seeds of flowering plants. This means many culinary vegetables, such as tomatoes, bean pods, okra, eggplants, peppers, and squash, are technically fruits.

narrativeText: All this hair was collected in Vancouver. The samples show the diversity of our population, but also that hair from one person looks more like each other's than hair from families. This is because a child's physical features are inherited from his or her parents...

narrativeText: Humans have discovered agriculture many times independently. Eastern North America is just one of at least eight places where scientists think farming emerged. One of the first crops to be domesticated there, thousands of years ago, was the sunflower. These varieties of sunflower were cultivated by Native Americans in the locations shown, and are still important today.

narrativeText: It took centuries of international trade and exploration to create the hamburger. Ingredients that were first domesticated in places as far away as Asia, the Mediterranean, and Central America had to come together before Canadians could finally taste this now well-known meal.

narrativeText: Most people know little about noug, an important oil-seed crop in Ethiopia, where it grows in places so wet that many other crops can't survive. Researchers are studying noug diversity and working with Ethiopian farmers and scientists to breed varieties of noug that produce more and are easier to farm. In Canada, noug seeds are known as "nyier" or "thistle seeds" and sold as food for finches.

narrativeText: Crop diversity protects our food supply by providing insurance against diseases, pests, changing climates, and other threats. But as agriculture globalizes, worldwide crop diversity is shrinking fast. To help preserve this diversity for future generations, thousands of varieties of seeds are stored in seed banks around the world. These metal envelopes store precious seeds at the national genebank in Saskatoon, Saskatchewan.

narrativeText: The seed evolved just once in history, and how lucky for us! Not only do seed plants dominate nearly every environment on land, but our civilization depends on them.

narrativeText: "The road we have long been traveling is deceptively easy, a smooth superhighway on which we progress with great speed, but at its end lies disaster. The other fork of the road—the one less traveled by—offers our last, our only chance to reach a destination that assures the preservation of the earth." —Rachel Carson, Silent Spring, 1962. "Hope is the thing with feathers / That perches in the soul, / And sings the tune—without the words, / And never stops at all." —Emily Dickinson, 1861. Alan Brelsford completed his PhD in UBC's Department of Zoology in 2010. His research used genetic markers to study the formation of new bird species. After the last ice age, two species began interbreeding in a contact zone in the Rocky Mountains. But researchers have found that even after hybridizing for thousands of years, the birds have still kept their distinct colour patterns.

narrativeText: Biodiversity "hot spots" are places that rank particularly high in number of species, number of rare species, intensity of threat, or some combination of the three. UBC researchers have detected three biodiversity hot spots in British Columbia: the South Okanagan, the Lower Mainland, and southeastern Vancouver Island plus the Gulf Islands. "In nature's economy the currency is not money, it is life." —Vandana Shiva, Earth Democracy, 2005. Plants in the genus Artemesia include sagebrush and wormwood. Geoffrey Scudder is a professor at UBC's Department of Zoology. He is a zoologist with special interest and expertise in systematics, biogeography, biodiversity, conservation biology, and evolution.

narrativeText: British Columbia boasts more biodiversity than anywhere else in Canada, with well over 42,500 animal species. We have an incredible range of habitats, and many unique species that can only be found here. In 2009, 44 species were endangered, which means they are facing imminent extirpation or extinction. The burrowing owl, native to the Okanagan, is endangered in British Columbia due to habitat loss. These fossilized seeds are all from the Clarno formation in Oregon. The tropical plants they came from flourished around the world during the Eocene Epoch, when the Earth was much warmer than it is now. Today, they live only in a few places with hot climates—none survive in the wild in Oregon. Early plants reproduced without seeds, like today's mosses do. Once pollinated and fertilized, most flowers develop fruits with seeds. Fruits come in different shapes and sizes, but each has the same job: to protect and help disperse the plant's seeds. Flowering plants use many means to disperse their fruit and seeds, such as wind, animals, water, or explosive capsules. This helps new seedlings grow far from their parents to avoid competing for water, light, and nutrients.

narrativeText: The Vancouver Island marmot is one of Canada's most endangered mammals. Although scientists aren't sure why their numbers have dropped, it probably has to do with changing predator/prey dynamics. But since 2003, a captive-breeding program has released over 300 marmots, and their numbers are recovering. Thank a pollinator for every third bite of food you eat. Pollination by insects, birds, and bats is responsible for about 30 per cent of the world's food supply. When crops are exposed to a variety and abundance of pollinators, food production soars. More pollinators equals more berries, fruits, vegetables, nuts, and even chocolate to satisfy your palate. Flowering plants use many means to disperse their fruit and seeds, such as wind, animals, water, or explosive capsules. This helps new seedlings grow far from their parents to avoid competing for water, light, and nutrients.

narrativeText: Snow goose populations are increasing at their summer breeding sites along Hudson Bay's shores. However, the geese are slowly eating themselves out of house and home. Overgrazing of their food plants has allowed the soil to dry out, causing buried marine salts to rise to the surface and preventing plants from revegetating the barren areas. Red areas show receding vegetation around La Perouse Bay, Manitoba. Can you guess which of these pollinators are found in British Columbia? Moss makes excellent building material because it is flexible and durable.

narrativeText: Fragments of habitat—even tiny moss patches—tend to have more species if they're connected to other fragments. When researchers removed the mites and springtails from moss patches, then gave the patches time to recover, they found that the ones connected by moss "corridors" ended up with more diversity. Mites from the surrounding patches used the corridors to recolonize the central, disturbed patch. Warming temperatures have benefited some butterfly species by allowing them to expand their range into new areas. Using museum specimens, researchers are mapping out how the eyed brown butterfly has expanded its range since 1900, moving northward as spring and summer seasons become warmer.

narrativeText: In 1986, a dam was built in Venezuela, flooding the forest and creating small islands. Researchers arrived seven years later and found the island ecosystems in turmoil. Predators like armadillos and army ants couldn't find enough food, so they disappeared. Their prey, the leaf-cutter ants, took over and stripped the forests bare. Some ecologists believe the same ecological cascade is happening around the world as top predators disappear.
notes: Large panoramic display panel. Armadillo specimen approximately 25x7in. Photo of flooded Venezuelan forest with dead trees. Reference ID D119 visible.

narrativeText: UBC researchers have studied tent caterpillar populations over several decades. The Oregon spotted frog is Canada's most endangered amphibian. Fewer than 300 remain, all in the Lower Mainland.

narrativeText: Some orcas eat higher on the food chain than others, preferring animals like seals and porpoises to salmon and squid. But this gourmet diet comes with a price: the mammals they eat deliver more concentrated doses of pollutants, so the orcas’ bodies are toxic waste dumps, contaminated with herbicides, pesticides, stain repellents, fire retardants, and detergents.
notes: Orca jaw bone specimen displayed. Label states 'To be accessioned' (no catalog number). Illustrations show orca food chain: orcas, seal, porpoise, salmon, fish, squid, with yellow dots indicating pollutant concentration levels. Artist credit: Sigal Friedmann. Old red-bordered paper label affixed to jaw bone (text illegible). Drawer label D121.

narrativeText: To better understand modern ecosystems, researchers uncover their past. Louise Blight analyses museum specimens dating from 1860 to find chemical changes in seabird feathers and fish muscles over time. These changes reveal alterations to marine food webs from fishing and climate change. Researchers often use seabirds as ecosystem monitors because they’re common and widespread.
notes: Glaucous-winged gull study skin, February 5, 1971, Tsawwassen, BC. Researcher profile: Louise Blight is a PhD candidate in the UBC Centre for Applied Conservation Research, where she uses stable isotope analysis to study marine food webs in coastal BC. University of British Columbia, 2007. Common poorwill wing specimen displayed separately with no catalog number visible. Line drawing of bird by David Gilbar visible. Drawer label D122.

narrativeText: Abalone shells are very sensitive to changes in the watery environment. When carbon dioxide in the atmosphere increases, oceans absorb it and become more acidic. And just as the acid in soda can erode the enamel of your teeth, too much acid in the water prevents calcium-based shells and coral skeletons from forming properly. Researchers raised these larvae in a lab to study the effects of ocean acidification.
notes: Abalone shells displayed in vertical case on right side. Three microscope images of larvae shown at different CO2 levels: 400 ppm CO2 (Year 2010 levels), 800 ppm CO2 (Year 2100 levels), 1,800 ppm CO2 (Year 2300 levels). *ppm = parts per million. September 2009, Bamfield Huu-ay-aht Community Abalone Project hatchery, Vancouver Island, BC. Photo credit: Ryan Crim. Researcher profile: Ryan Crim is a master’s student in UBC’s Department of Zoology. His passion for nature has led him to research how marine organisms are impacted by human disturbances. University of British Columbia, 2010. Shell dimensions noted as 6.5" x 10.5". Drawer label D123.

narrativeText: Plankton are tiny plants or animals that float in aquatic environments. Their abundance changes with the seasons. In spring, warming waters bring plankton up, where nutrients and sunlight cause phytoplankton—the plants—to explode with life. They grow so fast that the animals that eat them, such as krill, can’t keep up, causing a phytoplankton “bloom.”
notes: Krill specimen in jar displayed. Label: Krill, Euphausiacea, February 18, 1954, San Lucas, Baja California, Mexico. Jar dimensions noted as 3.5" x 4.5". Satellite image of phytoplankton bloom off the coast of Vancouver Island, June 25, 2006. Photo credit: Jeff Schmaltz, NASA. Drawer label D124.

narrativeText: Cowbirds are moving west. On British Columbia’s Gulf Islands, this has contributed to long-term declines of song sparrow populations. Why? Cowbirds don’t build nests. They leave their eggs in the nests of other birds, removing a host egg to make room. Human land-clearing has helped cowbirds expand their range to most of North America.
notes: Brown-headed cowbird eggs: 1950s, Grindrod, BC, No. 21873. Brown-headed cowbirds (two study skins): June 24, 1941, Okanagan, BC, No. 192 and No. 193. Song sparrow eggs: No. 21487 (no date/location on visible label). Song sparrow study skin #1: October 30, 1934, Okanagan Landing, BC, No. 6233. Song sparrow study skin #2: November 1, 1934, Okanagan Landing, BC, No. 6235. Nest photo captioned 'Cowbird eggs in vireo nest.' Photo credit: Conan Guard. Drawer label D125.

narrativeText: Mosses are special because they can dry out and curl up when it’s hot and dry, and come right back to life when the rains come. This feature allows them to live in many inhospitable locations, including the desert. This is a Syntrichia moss when wet. How does it look different from the dried specimen?
notes: Syntrichia ruralis dried specimen displayed: January 26, 2011, UBC campus, Vancouver, BC, No. E.0367. Photo of wet Syntrichia moss shown for comparison (credit: Judith Harper). Photo of desiccated Grimmia moss shown (credit: Stephanie Stuber). Caption: 'A dessicated Grimmia'. Drawer label D126.

narrativeText: As beautiful as the flowers of Scotch broom or the leaves of English ivy may be, these European plants are alien species in our local Garry oak ecosystem. Plants and animals that are introduced into and invade different ecosystems can do serious harm. They often compete aggressively with native species. Dinosaurs still roam... Actually, they fly-and you can see some in your backyard. Fossils reveal that birds probably evolved from theropod dinosaurs during the Jurassic. Birds and theropods both have four-toed feet and... Researchers raised these larvae in a lab to study the effects of ocean acidification. ...and coral skeletons from forming properly. 400 ppm CO2 Year 2010 levels. 800 ppm CO2 Year 2100 levels. 1,800 ppm CO2 Year 2300 levels. *ppm = parts per million.

narrativeText: Canada geese are helping invasive plants spread in British Columbia's Gulf Islands. When they eat exotic grasses like these, those seeds survive in their intestinal tract. When they fly over to the islands, they scatter the seeds, creating new populations of the plants. Did you know Canada geese aren't actually native to British Columbia? Some orcas eat higher on the food chain... others, preferring animals like porpoises to salmon and squid... gourmet diet comes with a price... mammals they eat deliver more...

Contains Canada goose illustration, Yellow Sweetgrass herbarium specimen (label partially legible — Savage Island, BC), orca illustration with yellow dots, vertebra fossil marked '543', large fossil bone/rock with red octagonal label (illegible), brachiopod fossils in box, trilobite fossil (pyrite replacement) from Cambrian, Antelope Springs, Utah (No. 2008-405), fossil excavation photo from Drummond Montana (credit: Craig Christensen), chisel kit, ruler, soil with tools. narrativeText: Don't turn your back on these invasive plants! In British Columbia, we're waging war by pulling them out of the ground, releasing insects that eat them, ensuring we don't transport their seeds on clothing and equipment, and not planting them in garden beds. These fossilized seeds are all from the Clarno formation in Oregon. The tropical plants they came from flourished around the world during the Eocene Epoch, when the Earth was much warmer than it is now. Today, they live only in a few places with hot climates — none survive in the wild in Oregon. Many Eocene plants did persist into modern times on the Pacific Coast — these seeds may be related to modern blackberries.
notes: Multi-topic display panel. Contains three invasive plant herbarium specimens (Policeman's helmet from Charles Rummel Park Burnaby BC, Japanese knotweed from UBC campus Vancouver BC, Purple loosestrife from Vancouver BC July 2010), three Eocene fossil seeds from Clarno Formation Oregon, two herbarium specimens (Leadwood and Gentian from UBC Botanical Gardens Aug 16 2010). Tropical vegetation photo. Text at bottom: '...visit 2. F. May visit 1. G. May visit 2.' with number '4'. D178 visible. Catalog numbers for invasive plants partially illegible.

narrativeText: Hunted to near-extinction for their luscious pelts, sea otters have fought an uphill battle since the fur trade began in the late 1700s. By the late 1960s, fewer than 1000 survived, in Alaska and California, before otters were re-introduced to southeast Alaska, British Columbia, Washington, and Oregon. Now, with their populations growing rapidly, researchers are studying the relationships among otters, shellfish, kelp forests, and people. Thank a pollinator for every third bite of food you eat. Pollination by insects, birds, and bats is responsible for about 30 per cent of the world's food supply. When crops are exposed to a variety and abundance of pollinators, food production soars. More pollinators equals more berries, fruits, vegetables, nuts, and even chocolate to satisfy your palate. ...dozens of species, and even produce kinds of toxins. ...aete worms eaten by ...to only one family, the Eunicidae.

Pedaliaceae, sunflower seeds in F. Asteraceae, watermelon seeds partially visible), polychaete worm illustration (credit: Elin Sigvaldadottir), millipede/centipede line drawings, cone shells (7 visible), fish skeleton/fin bones. Rebecca Martone bio: postdoctoral associate in UBC's Institute for Resources, Environment and Sustainability, researching effects of otter reintroduction on kelp forests off Vancouver Island (University of British Columbia, 2010). D166 visible. Purple sea urchin from San Juan Island, Puget Sound, British Columbia. 'Using rocks, otters break open tough sea urchins before eating them.'

narrativeText: Otters are called a keystone species because they have a strong influence on the biodiversity of nearshore marine ecosystems by gorging on invertebrates — up to five kilograms a day! Open the next drawer to find out what happens when these important predators disappear. Native edible plants abound in Vancouver. Huckleberries, salmonberries, and thimbleberries produce edible fruits, and Siberian miner's lettuce makes a great addition to salads. Even nettles are tasty when lightly cooked to disarm the sting! Female tarantula hawk wasp (Pepsis formosa) paralyzing a tarantula.
notes: Multi-topic display panel. Contains four native edible plant herbarium specimens (Red huckleberry from Hunter Park Langley BC July 26 2011, Salmonberry from UBC campus Vancouver BC Aug 8 2011, Thimbleberry from Hunter Park Langley BC July 26 2011, Siberian miner's lettuce from Wreck Beach Trail 6 Vancouver BC May 18 2011), tarantula hawk wasp specimen (Pepsini, July 21 1998, Nixon Springs Mount Trumbull Mojave County Arizona), tarantula (Theraphosidae, Teaching collection), sea otter skull, sea urchin test, purple sea urchin label (Strongylocentrotus purpuratus, San Juan Island Puget Sound British Columbia, E.0197 a,b), cooked nettles photos (credits: Tim Parkinson, Lauren Fan), sea otter photo. Rebecca Martone bio. D210 visible. ~4x7.75in dimension notation. 'Using rocks, otters break open tough sea urchins before eating them.' BC sea otter population graph.

narrativeText: Kelps create underwater forests, the ultimate buffet for marine life off the BC coast, where diverse collections of animals benefit from the three-dimensional habitat and rich production of food they provide. But sea urchins dramatically reduce kelp forests if there aren't enough otters to keep these kelp-eating herbivores in check. When sea urchins mow down kelp forests, scientists call what's left behind "urchin barrens." These little brown wrens, which look almost identical but sing very different songs, are actually two different species. Evidence suggests that they split apart over four million years ago, when one population was separated into two different areas. But both occur in Tumbler Ridge, BC, and UBC research in this contact zone has shown that they don't interbreed. The Pacific Wren was officially recognized as a species in July 2010, as a result of UBC research.
notes: Multi-topic display panel. Contains kelp herbarium specimen (Macrocystis integrifolia, May 22 1999, Bamfield Vancouver Island BC, catalog number partially illegible), Pacific wren study skin, Winter wren study skin, range map showing Pacific wren (red, western) and Winter wren (blue, eastern) distributions with Tumbler Ridge contact zone marked, urchin barren photo. Dr. Russell Markel bio: post-doctoral fellow in UBC's Institute for Resources Environment and Sustainability, studies how over-exploitation of predators impacts food webs in marine environments (University of British Columbia, 2010). David Toews bio: PhD student in Darren Irwin's research group in UBC Department of Zoology, studying how new species of birds form and adapt to different environments (University of British Columbia, 2010). D070 visible. 6.5" x 5" dimension notation. Red herbarium label on kelp specimen partially legible.

narrativeText: Warming temperatures have benefited some butterfly species by allowing them to expand their range into new areas. Using museum specimens, researchers are mapping out how the eyed brown butterfly has expanded its range since 1900, moving northward as spring and summer seasons become warmer.
notes: Six numbered butterfly specimens (1-6) displayed in vertical column. Label reads 'Eyed brown butterflies / Eurydice satyrodes'. Specimen 1: July 7, 1980, Ridout Township, Cascade Tree Farm, Muskoka District, Ontario, No. E.0291. Specimen 2: May 14, 1977, Atikokan, Rainy River District, Ontario, No. E.0292. Specimen 3: July 29, 1979, Wiarton, Bruce County, Ontario, No. E.0293. Specimen 4: July 18, 1980, Rondeau Park, Ontario, No. E.0294. She studies how climate change in Canada affects overall diversity, especially butterflies and their host plants. University of British Columbia, 2010. Panel labeled D135.

narrativeText: Even a tiny beetle can wreak havoc on a forest. Native to British Columbia, mountain pine beetle populations were kept in check because cold winter temperatures killed most of them. Since 2000, warming winters and drier summers have meant exploding populations and the destruction of millions of trees.
notes: Mountain pine beetle, Dendroctonus ponderosae, July 2009, Bear Forestry Service Road, near Merritt, BC, No. E.0309 a-c. Three pinned beetle specimens shown with handwritten collection labels. Large landscape photograph of beetle-killed forest (red/brown dead trees among green) credited Tim Gage. Additional text: 'Beetle-killed trees still make good construction material. The picnic benches outside the museum are made of reclaimed beetle-killed wood.' Photo of students at picnic benches by Nigel Freedman. Panel labeled D136.

narrativeText: Vancouver Island white-tailed ptarmigan change colour with the seasons to blend in with their alpine habitat: mottled brown in summer, white in winter. But erratic weather patterns associated with climate change can leave them exposed and vulnerable, their body camouflage at odds with their habitat.
notes: Study skin specimen displayed (mottled brown summer plumage), size 6"x14". Photo of white ptarmigan in alpine habitat with patchy snow, credited Steve Ogle. Caption: 'This white ptarmigan has difficulty blending with its habitat when winter snowfall is late.' Researcher profile: Kathy Martin is a professor in UBC's Department of Forest Sciences. She studies the effects of climate change on alpine birds. University of British Columbia, 2010. Photo by Jamie Smith. Panel labeled D137.

narrativeText: Snowshoe hare and lynx populations go through dramatic cycles every 10 to 11 years. Researchers discovered that the ebb and flow of the two populations are closely linked. When predation by lynx reduces the number of hares too much, the lynx will either starve or move in search of more prey.
notes: Canada lynx skull displayed, size 6"x 7". Population cycle graph showing Lynx and Hare populations over Years (oscillating curves). Researcher profile: Charles J. Krebs is a professor emeritus in UBC's Department of Zoology. For 10 years, he and his team tagged and counted lynx and hares at Kluane National Park. University of British Columbia, 2010. Panel labeled D138.

narrativeText: Named after their large, furry feet, snowshoe hares are a food staple for the lynx. Other predators, like foxes and coyotes, also rely on them for food in the Kluane boreal forest. The hares, in turn, eat the tips of branches of shrubs and trees.
notes: Snowshoe hare study skin displayed (white winter pelage), 1932, Mons, BC, No. 6040, size 7.5" x 21". Skull also displayed separately. Food web diagram showing predators of the hare: Wolf, Coyote, Hawk, Wolverine, Red squirrel, Fox, Owl, Lynx. Food sources consumed by hare: Grey willow, Bog birch, Grasses, White spruce, Soapberry, Ground berries. Panel labeled D139.

narrativeText: Lynx feed on squirrels, voles, and reindeer, though snowshoe hares are their favourite prey. Their diet is up to 98 per cent dependent on the hare, but lynx will eat ground squirrels and ptarmigan if hares are rare. And even the lynx has predators to fear.
notes: Spruce grouse study skin displayed with specimen tag reading 3789 and Falcipennis canadensis osgoodi, size 6.5" x 16". Ground squirrel study skin displayed, size 6.5" x 16". Food web diagram showing Lynx (central, highlighted) with prey arrows to Snowshoe hare, Squirrel, and Vole, and predator arrows from Wolf, Wolverine, and Coyote. Panel labeled D140.

narrativeText: Owls are carnivores (meat-eaters) at the top of the food chain. By dissecting their pellets, we can learn what they are eating. Large owls can kill mammals like rabbits and skunks. While smaller owls eat birds and shrews, and small invertebrates like insects. | Owls and the entomology section help with pollination. | All this hair was collected in Vancouver.
notes: Composite image of multiple exhibit display panels. Contains bird specimens with labels, a pollination/entomology section with a photograph of a beetle on yellow flowers and a pressed plant specimen, an owl in flight, and panels about algae, food crops (seeds including quinoa/Chenopodium quinoa in Chenopodiaceae, and barley/Hordeum vulgare in Poaceae), and seabirds as ecosystem monitors. Some text is rotated/angled and partially illegible. Spotted towhee collected March 18, 1970, UBC Endowment Lands, Vancouver, BC. Glaucous-winged gull collected February 5, 1971, Tsawwassen, BC.

narrativeText: Songbirds are disappearing from North American forests. Habitat loss is one issue, but researchers at UBC have also shown that overpopulation by deer can have an effect. Deer browse down low-lying vegetation, sometimes doing away with it all together. In so doing, they eliminate habitat for some songbird species. | Strong aromas like pine, citrus, and mint all come from one group of plant chemicals, called terpenes, often found in tree bark. Many of these chemicals have been shown to ward off insects. Whatever its use to the plant, the terpene taxol, from the bark of Pacific yew, helps fight cancer. Taxol is now synthesized in the lab, sparing wild populations of Pacific yew.
notes: Composite image. Top panel: songbirds disappearing, shows bird specimens (yellow warbler, spotted towhee or similar), feather, and forest understory comparison photos (Flattop Island with no deer vs Sidney Island with high deer density). Researcher credit: Tara Martin, Australia's Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation (CSIRO) and adjunct professor at UBC's Department of Forest Sciences. Bottom panel: terpenes and Pacific yew. Taxus sp. collected August 8, 2011, UBC campus, Vancouver, BC. Pacific yew bark collected August 7, 2011, Emerald Forest Conservation Area, Whistler, BC. Panel code D213 visible. Derek Tan illustration credit.

narrativeText: Loss of a single species can cause other species that depend on it to also go extinct. But what happens when you lose multiple species? Researchers studying bromeliad food webs found that simultaneously removing top predators and some of their prey led to less predictable extinctions in distant parts of the food web. Damselfly larvae are top predators in the bromeliad food web. Removing insect species like damselflies and tipulids caused many ciliate species to go extinct, even though they were many links away in the food web. Tiny, contained ecosystems exist in the pools of water between bromeliads' leaves. | Plants in the genus Artemesia include sagebrush and wormwood (an ingredient in absinthe). Another species produces artemisinin, a potent anti-malarial drug. Used for over a thousand years, artemesinin gained international recognition in the 1980s. Today, the drug is so widely used that malarial parasites are developing resistance.
notes: Composite image. Top-left: bromeliad food web panel with Diane Srivastava (UBC Department of Zoology). Middle-bottom: wormwood specimen and Artemisia narrative. Bottom-right: graptolite fossil from Ledbetter Formation, Ordovician, Rice, Washington. Photo of sagebrush by David Gilbar.

narrativeText: Fish or meat? UBC researchers working in West Africa found a link between market prices of fish and bushmeat — meat from illegally hunted wild animals. Buyers favour fish, but when the price of fish goes up, more people buy bushmeat, putting pressure on these threatened populations. | Coral reefs are beautiful, marine battleground where corals compete with neighbours. | The Age of Sail, which allowed humans to have global impacts for the first time, couldn't have happened without hemp. Cultivated for thousands of years, by the 16th century, cannabis provided cheap, durable hemp fibre for ropes and centuries of worldwide trade in people and goods.
notes: Composite image of multiple panels. Vervet monkey skull displayed, from Hobok District, Uganda. Lopha cristagalli displayed on Spondylus multimuricatus. Chama sp. also visible (No. MI411_, partially cut off). Trilobite from Harrigan Formation, Devonian, Oklahoma. Tentaculites sp. from Perdix Shales, Rocky Mountains (No. 2010-7592). Another fossil visible with No. 2010-7590 (partially cut off, possibly Upper Devonian, Scotland). Also includes graph showing estimated hunting intensity vs regional per capita fish supply, and cross-section diagrams of shoreline ecosystems.

narrativeText: Flamingos wouldn't be pink if they didn't eat blue-green algae and brine shrimp. Flamingos turn carotenoids from their food into hot-pink pigment for their feathers. Different species are different shades of pink depending on what they eat. Zoo-kept birds are white unless they are fed the appropriate food or supplement. | Dipterus is related to modern-day lungfishes, which can breathe air. | Piercing and sucking mouthparts: True bugs have beaklike mouths to feed on liquids in plants or prey. Female mosquitoes use needlelike mouthparts to extract blood from vertebrates.
notes: Composite image. Top: flamingo panel with photo, brine shrimp specimen (Artemia salina), and American flamingo feather (Phoenicopterus ruber). Plant life cycle diagram showing Growth, Flowering and pollination, Seed structure (Endosperm, Embryo, Seed coat, Pericarp). Blue corn specimen. Insect mouthparts panel with SEM image of fly. Fish fossil (Dipterus macrolepidotus) from Old Red Sandstone, Upper Triassic, Scotland (partial text: lepidotus visible). Coral (Scleractinia). Gastropod from Middle Devonian, Rolla Quadrangle, Missouri. Fossil with handwritten label 'Planotis subcircularis Peace R.' also visible. Photo credit: Justin Beck (bee on flower).

narrativeText: To compete with other predators, this snail hunts with specialized toxins and eats just three worm species. But near Easter Island, it has fewer competitors, so you'd think it could eat more kinds of food — and researchers found that it does! There, it eats dozens of species, and even produces more kinds of toxins. The three species of polychaete worms eaten by most Conus miliaris belong to only one family, the Eunicidae. | Most adult insects sport two pairs of wings. Notice how some wings are delicate and transparent, and others are solid and colourful. These and other differences can help you distinguish one insect group from another. True flies have one pair of wings.
notes: Composite image. Top: cone snail panel with multiple Conus shells displayed and radular teeth specimens. Catalog number partially visible (No. MI4[...]). Polychaete worm illustration. Bottom: insect wings panel showing insect order labels — Flies (Diptera), Beetles (Coleoptera), Grasshoppers (Orthoptera), Bees (Hymenoptera), Butterflies/moths (Lepidoptera), Dragonflies (Odonata). Close-up moth wing photo by Timothy Lauro. Mosquito illustration by Derek Tan. Panel code D160.

narrativeText: If spiders have nightmares, they're probably about wasps called tarantula hawks. When the female wasp is ready to breed, she paralyzes a tarantula with venom and lays an egg. The larva burrows into the spider and eats it from the inside, avoiding the vital organs — to make sure its food stays fresh. | It's time to test your knowledge. narrativeText: "In nature's economy the currency is not money, it is life." – Vandana Shiva, Earth Democracy, 2005. The small pools of water that collect inside bromeliads can host complex webs of interactions among organisms.

narrativeText: Although this parrot isn't endangered, its trade is regulated by international law, like that of most parrots. Parrots face a dual threat: habitat loss and the pet trade have combined to endanger 94 of the world's parrot species. Thousands of animals are still taken from sensitive habitats every year, but captive breeding programs have helped to relieve the pressure in some places.

narrativeText: Each year up to 20 million seahorses are caught and traded for traditional medicine, aquariums, and curios. Traditional Chinese medicine is the largest market — seahorses are believed to help with many conditions, including asthma, impotency, incontinence, and poor circulation. In 2004 international trade in seahorses and their products was restricted in an effort to conserve populations.

narrativeText: Using guns and grenades, poachers often kill elephants just for their tusks. This jewellery has a high hidden cost: the trade of precious corals and ivory threatens elephant herds and coral reefs worldwide. Although some trade is legal, it's impossible to know where a piece of ivory or coral came from, so it's best not to buy any at all.

narrativeText: Licorice ferns are epiphytes: plants that grow on other plants and obtain nutrients from the air, rain, through-fall, and outer bark rather than the soil. Licorice ferns often hang out on bigleaf maples, along with a whole community of other epiphytes like moss and lichens. In the most rain-soaked places of the Pacific coast, epiphytes can weigh nearly four times as much as the leaves of their host maples.

narrativeText: In the water-filled leaves of the meat-eating pitcher plant, you'll find a whole community of organisms that rely on each other. As they feed, midge larvae break down the carcasses of insects that have fallen in. Bacteria colonize these body fragments and are eaten by other microscopic creatures, which are then filtered from the water by mosquito larvae.

narrativeText: Flowering plants entice insects to deliver their pollen to suitable plant mates. You're probably familiar with the advertising strategy. Their showy, often fragrant flowers attract insects that then collect a reward, such as nectar, found inside. It took millions of years for the insects and flowers to work out these special relationships called coevolutionary mutualisms. Pollen gets stuck to insects' bodies as they move from flower to flower.

narrativeText: These fossilized seeds are all from the Clarno formation in Oregon. The tropical plants they came from flourished around the world during the Eocene Epoch, when the Earth was much warmer than it is now. Today, they live only in a few places with hot climates — none survive in the wild in Oregon. Many Eocene plants did persist into modern times on the Pacific Coast — these seeds may be related to modern blackberries. All insects are arthropods, but not all arthropods are insects. Can you find the insects in this group?

narrativeText: From familiar to bizarre, an insect's mouthparts can provide clues to what it eats. Chewing mouthparts: Insects with broad, flat jaws, such as grasshoppers, eat mostly plants, while those with knifelike jaws, like beetles, eat anything they can chew, tear, cut, or crush. Siphoning mouthparts: Butterflies and moths have long, slender, coiled proboscises that extend to suck up flower nectar. Sponging mouthparts: Fly mouthparts sponge up liquids. To eat solid food, flies vomit up saliva to dissolve it first. Mmm... Piercing and sucking mouthparts: True bugs have beaklike mouths to feed on liquids in plants or prey. Female mosquitoes use needlelike mouthparts to extract blood from vertebrates.
notes: Display panel about insect mouthpart types with SEM images. Crops bleed into adjacent marine invertebrate display. narrativeText: Most adult insects sport two pairs of wings. Notice how some wings are delicate and transparent, and others are solid and colourful. These and other differences can help you distinguish one insect group from another. True flies have one pair of wings. Seed dispersal: Blown by wind, Eaten by animals, Hitchhikers on animal fur, feathers, or claws, Self-dispersed (expelled from exploding seedpods). You can think of moss communities as miniature forests. Under the green canopy is a protected habitat for organisms such as mites, springtails, sheltered from environmental extremes. They are important food for creatures higher up.

narrativeText: Life on Earth depends on plants and pollinators. To reproduce, many plants need insects, birds, and other small creatures to spread their pollen from plant to plant, and the insects benefit from the tasty rewards offered by the flowers. Pollination allows seeds to grow and develop into new plants, which can become food for people and other animals.

narrativeText: It took millions of years for insects and flowers to develop these special relationships, called mutualisms. Fig wasps crawl inside figs to lay their eggs, pollinating the tiny flowers. This relationship has been evolving for 80 million years.

narrativeText: Certain plants and insects have shaped one another's evolution in dramatic ways. For instance, stinky flowers appeal to dung-loving flies, flowers with trap doors only open for bees of a certain weight, and rough-textured flowers help bumblebees get a grip. Honeysuckle's slender tubes are perfect nectar vessels for the tongue of the local bumblebee moth. Markings on foxglove petals provide a "landing strip" for insects that see ultraviolet light. Insects are the most diverse group of animals in your backyard -- and the easiest to meet. But how well do you know them? Look closely: how many kinds of bees can you count? Can you find a true bug that feeds on the juices of a plant? Which of these insects would you most likely find near water? Is there one you've never seen before?

narrativeText: Orchids, buttercups, lilies, and roses aren't just for the garden. In our local forests, plants from these families and dozens more in a myriad of colours and shapes await the careful observer in spring and summer. Conifer trees are the scaffolding of Vancouver's landscape. Our most common native tree in Vancouver is Douglas fir, which you can identify by its needles: they are attached individually instead of in bundles, and they spiral around the branches. Ferns and horsetails, like conifers, reproduce without flowers and are abundant in our woods. Most plants with showy flowers are called angiosperms. Animals with showy flowers that attract animals for pollination and tasty fruit for seed dispersal have been a great success. Animals and plants have diversified together for almost 140 million years.

Most people think about bees when it comes to pollination. But about 1,000 species of hummingbirds, bats, mice, and other small birds and mammals act as pollinators too. Native edible plants abound in Vancouver. Huckleberries, salmonberries, and thimbleberries produce edible fruits, and Siberian miner's lettuce makes a great addition to salads. Even nettles are tasty when lightly cooked to disarm the sting!

narrativeText: Jack Sepkoski and David Raup analyzed diversity in fossil species to identify the "big five" mass extinctions. Five mass extinctions, where more than 30 per cent of species disappear in a short period of time, have occurred in the past 540 million years. Mass extinctions occur when an ecosystem is so stressed that the number of species going extinct is not matched by the evolution of new species. Insects have three body sections. You can see them in this locust. Reefs such as this one, dominated by corals, disappeared during the Permian and didn't reappear until 10 million years later. Geoffrey Scudder is a professor emeritus in UBC's Department of Zoology. He is a zoologist with special interest and experience in entomology, biosystematics, biogeography, biodiversity, conservation biology, and evolution.

narrativeText: The end-Ordovician extinction consisted of two events between 450 and 440 million years ago, when all known life on Earth lived in the oceans. A large land mass drifted into the south polar region, making temperatures plummet, ice form, and sea levels fall, destroying ocean habitat. About two-thirds of marine invertebrates disappeared, including two-thirds of all brachiopod and bryozoan families. Complete metamorphosis. About 88 per cent of insects grow this way. The larvae are eating machines, the pupae are transformers, and the adults are designed for reproduction. Incomplete metamorphosis. About 12 per cent of insects grow this way. Nymphs shed their outer skins many times before reaching adulthood.

narrativeText: The late Devonian extinction, 360 to 375 million years ago, hit the oceans hard. It ravaged coral reefs and wiped out all but two trilobite orders. Sea level drops and ocean anoxia could have been the main causes. This armoured fish, among the first with jaws, went extinct during the Devonian. Dipterus is related to modern-day lungfishes, which can breathe air. Crop diversity protects our food supply by providing insurance against diseases, pests, changing climates, and other threats. But as agriculture globalizes, worldwide crop diversity is shrinking fast. To help preserve this diversity for future generations, thousands of varieties of seeds are stored in seed banks around the world. These metal envelopes store precious seeds at the national genebank in Saskatoon, Saskatchewan. Ken W. Richards is the research manager for the Canadian Genetic Resource Program in Saskatoon. He directs Canada's genetic resources program for plant, animal, and microbial diversity.

narrativeText: Earth lost a lot of diversity during the Triassic-Jurassic extinction, about 200 million years ago — at least half of the species known to be living then. That loss was the dinosaurs' gain, though. They got room to roam, and they dominated later, in the Jurassic. Life's not always easy for mosses in the canopy. In summer, they have to be able to withstand both drying out and the leaves of their host tree blocking the sun. But in autumn, the leaves fall and the rains come, bringing sunlight and water so the moss can thrive again.

narrativeText: Too tough for T-rex? You may have heard of the Cretaceous-Tertiary extinction, about 65 million years ago, because it was the end of non-flying dinosaurs. There's debate about the cause, but in 2010 a panel of scientists agreed it was the Chicxulub asteroid impact, found in Mexico, that drove most dinosaurs to extinction (a few survived to evolve into modern-day birds). Flowering plants use many means to disperse their fruit and seeds, such as wind, animals, water, or explosive capsules. This helps new seedlings grow far from their parents to avoid competing for water, light, and nutrients.

narrativeText: There rolls the deep where grew the tree / O earth, what changes hast thou seen! / There where the long street roars hath been / The stillness of the central sea. — Alfred, Lord Tennyson, "In Memoriam A.H.H.," 1850. O earth, what changes hast thou seen! Whitebark pine relies heavily on the Clark’s nutcracker for seed dispersal. Each bird can cache up to 100,000 seeds each year. The life-support systems hidden in the abdomen allow an insect to breathe, digest, and reproduce. All insects have six legs. Notice how all three pairs of legs extend from the thorax. narrativeText: Fish or meat? UBC researchers working in West Africa found a link between market prices of fish and bushmeat — meat from illegally hunted wild animals. Buyers favour fish, but when the price of fish goes up, more people buy bushmeat, putting pressure on these threatened populations. narrativeText: These fossilized seeds are all from the Clarno formation in Oregon. The tropical plants they came from flourished around the world during the Eocene Epoch, when the Earth was much warmer than it is now. Today, they live only in a few places with hot climates — none survive in the wild in Oregon. Many Eocene plants did persist into modern times on the Pacific Coast — these seeds may be related to modern blackberries. Early plants reproduced without seeds, like today’s mosses do. Land plants reproduce using sperm and eggs. For 150 million years, sperm had to swim through water to reach the egg, so plants had to reproduce close to the moist ground. Pollen and seeds changed all that. Pollen carries sperm through the air, and seeds protect and nourish young embryos.

narrativeText: Too hot or too cold? Many animals have disappeared as the climate has changed. Look at the mammoth, which once roamed North America. As the Earth grew warmer 12,000 years ago, their habitat shrank, and their numbers plummeted. But most scientists agree that hunting by humans also had a role in the animals’ demise. This mammoth tooth would have munched through tonnes of prehistoric leaves. Does it look very worn out to you?

narrativeText: Ammonites ruled the seas for about 200 million years, but their diversity plummeted at the end of the Triassic, when the number of genera shrank from over 100 to three. The ammonites rebounded within three million years, but full restoration of their diversity took 10 times that long. They finally became extinct at the end of the Cretaceous. Louise Longridge is a post-doctoral fellow in paleontology at UBC. So far she has unearthed 15 new species of ammonites in BC.

narrativeText: This humble tongue-shaped seed fern inspired a eureka moment for Victorian geologist Eduard Suess. Glossopteris is found in Permian rocks on all the southern continents — evidence that at one time, all these continents fit together, and have since drifted apart. Suess called this supercontinent “Gondwanaland.” Scientists now call it Gondwana. These little brown wrens, which look almost identical but singing very different songs, are actually two different species. Evidence suggests that they split apart over four million years ago, when one population was separated into two different areas. But both occur in Tumbler Ridge, BC, and UBC research in this contact zone has shown that they don't interbreed. The Pacific Wren was officially recognized as a species in July 2010, as a result of UBC research.

narrativeText: This fossil is about 50 million years old, but its modern relatives are sometimes called “living fossils.” Some people give the name to organisms, like this dawn redwood, that have survived over a long span of geological history with little change. These cone-bearing trees still live today in China. Each year up to 20 million seahorses are caught and traded for traditional medicine, aquariums, and curios. Traditional Chinese medicine is the largest market — seahorses are believed to help with many conditions, including asthma, impotency, incontinence, and poor circulation. In 2004 international trade in seahorses and their products was restricted in an effort to conserve populations.

narrativeText: The edible bulbs of camas are an important part of the traditional diets of coastal First Nations. Ecological research at UBC has shown that, until the arrival of Europeans, many coastal BC landscapes were actually carefully managed by fire to maintain open oak savannas where food plants like camas could thrive. Propagating seeds from camas and other native wildflowers can help restore their populations.
notes: Multi-panel panoramic image. Primary panel shows Camas specimens and narrative. narrativeText: The discovery of common periwinkles in Stanley Park was an important find, because these large marine snails could alter British Columbia's shorelines. They crowd out native snails and devour algae, undermining the foundation of intertidal ecosystems.
notes: Multi-panel panoramic image. Left panel shows Saskatoon berry with narrative: 'How many varieties of Saskatoon berry live in the BC Interior? Scientists recognize only one. But the St'at'imc (Lillooet) and Nlaka'pamux (Thompson) Interior Salish peoples recognize as many as five varieties, based on their close observation and use of this plant over the centuries. The specimens from Nancy Turner's ethnobotanical research on Saskatoon berry are held in the Beaty Museum.' Label shows (s)washlhowa7u7sa7 or 'red-berries' variety. Researcher: Nancy Turner, distinguished professor at University of Victoria's School of Environmental Studies, studied role of plants in traditional knowledge systems for over 40 years. Right panels show periwinkle species with map of Stanley Park and Vancouver area. Invasive vs Native sections indicated. Researcher: Chris Harley, associate professor in UBC's Department of Zoology, discovered common periwinkles in Stanley Park in 2010. Display code D031 visible. Catalog number v.l.d.014 visible on Saskatoon berry herbarium sheet.

narrativeText: Herring were once abundant along the BC coast, forming a cornerstone of human and ecological communities. But their populations have collapsed from commercial overfishing. Scientists are now turning to First Nations' traditional ecological knowledge and archaeological evidence to help determine past population numbers, so that we can better understand how to bring herring back.
notes: Multi-panel panoramic image. Top-left panel: 'Bones from archaeological sites can help us understand the former abundance of herring.' Shows Pacific herring (bones) specimen labeled 'Pacific herring (bones) / Clupea harengus pallasii / On loan from UBC Laboratory of Archaeology'. Historical photo captioned: 'Cedar boughs, laden with herring roe (foreground), and racks of herring were set out to dry near Sechelt Bay in Sliammon traditional territory. This photo, identified by Sliammon elder Mary George as her parents' backyard in 1939, shows a community practice that had continued for at least 900 years.' Researcher: Dana Lepofsky, professor at Simon Fraser University's Department of Archaeology, coordinates the 'Herring School' collaboration between scientists and people from First Nations communities working on herring research and conservation. Adjacent panel shows insect specimen preparation: 'We use delicate techniques for delicate specimens. Insects are dried and mounted on pins, and tiny labels attached. Sealed in boxes to keep out pests, specimens can last for hundreds of years.' Shows Schmidt box, insect kill jar, alcohol vials, insect spreading board and pins, D.F. Tayler & Co. entomological pins, Spencer Entomological Museum / Department of Zoology / University of British Columbia box. Display code D011 visible.

narrativeText: The Kwakwaka'wakw of the central BC coast harvest and dry the alga Porphyra abbottiae each year in late spring, along with halibut, eelgrass, and other marine species. It was once used as a medicine for goitre and traded with nations in the BC Interior. Today, it's used to manage the symptoms of diabetes and eaten as a tasty treat.

narrativeText: The Age of Sail, which allowed humans to have global impacts for the first time, couldn't have happened without hemp. Cultivated for nearly 5,000 years, by the 16th century, cannabis provided enough cheap, durable hemp fibre for ropes and sails to enable centuries of worldwide trade in people and goods. Varieties cultivated for fibre are different than cannabis cultivated for medicinal properties. These fossils from the Clarno formation in Oregon... tropical plants they came from... the world during the Eocene... Earth was much warmer than it is... survive only in a few places with hot... survive in the wild in Oregon. Many Eocene plants did persist into modern times on the Pacific Coast — these seeds may be related to modern blackberries.

narrativeText: At one time worth its weight in gold, pepper was traded across thousands of miles through Asia, Africa, and Europe. When the fall of Constantinople (modern-day Istanbul) disrupted these routes in 1453, the race to find a new spice route led to the European discovery of the Americas, and attempts to grow spices elsewhere in the tropics.

narrativeText: A gin and tonic was serious business if you were a British colonialist. Quinine, once the active ingredient in tonic water, was for centuries the only known treatment for malaria. Extracted from the bark of the South American cinchona tree, quinine was first used by Europeans in the 17th century. Access to quinine hastened colonial expansion and helped with the establishment of the British Empire. Abalone shells are very sensitive to changes in the watery environment. When carbon dioxide in the atmosphere increases, oceans absorb it and become more acidic. And just as the acid in soda can erode the enamel of your teeth, too much acid in the water prevents calcium-based shells and coral skeletons from forming properly. ...at changes hast thou seen!

narrativeText: Coffee could be dangerous, at least if you were an 18th-century monarch. Coffeehouses, where members of different social classes mingled as equals, were hotbeds of seditious thinking during the Enlightenment — particularly in America, where drinking coffee instead of British tea became a patriotic act. The Boston Tea Party was planned in a coffeehouse, and the Declaration of Independence was first pronounced in another. Complete metamorphosis: ...development of insects... the larvae are... the pupae are... the adults are... reproduction. Incomplete metamorphosis: About 12 per cent of insects grow this way. narrativeText: The genus Ginkgo has lived on Earth for 200 million years, but only one species survives today. Many people take ginkgo to help with memory, but scientific studies don't seem to support the plant's benefits in healthy people. However, it does show promise in treating people with dementia.
notes: Pressed ginkgo branch specimen collected August 4, 2011, UBC Botanical Garden, Vancouver, BC. Decorative ginkgo leaf line illustration on left. Photo of ginkgo tree credited to Tony Austin.

narrativeText: "Cotton is King" is no understatement for a plant whose profits sparked the Industrial Revolution, the London Stock Exchange, the Atlantic slave trade, and the colonization of the southeastern United States. On the eve of the American Civil War, over three million slaves worked in American cotton, tobacco, and sugar fields.
notes: Single-panel display (D197) showing world map with triangular trade routes (cotton from Americas to Europe, manufactured goods from Europe to Africa, enslaved people from Africa to Americas). narrativeText: Called "the queen of poisons," this monkshood has been a weapon of choice for assassins dating back to Medea, of Greek mythology. So powerful is aconitine, the deadly neurotoxin the plant produces, it was rumoured in the Middle Ages that unhappy wives would wash their husbands' shirts in a monkshood brew to commit undetectable murder.
notes: Overview photograph spanning multiple display panels. Visible panel codes: D033, D189. Features a monkshood (Aconitum) botanical specimen, a softshell clam shell in a petri dish with caption 'Notice the distinctive large hinge at the end of the softshell clam's shell', a slough sedge specimen from Jordan River BC (2010), and a profile of Pakki Chipps-Sawyer (PhD, University of Victoria, 2011) who studies ethnobotany and is a status member of the Beecher Bay First Nation in Sooke, BC. Also references 'Seed cones' and text about traditional collecting and timber industry changes. Additional narrative text about introduced species: 'Are all introduced species bad? Not always. Manila, varnish, and softshell clams have spread without noticeably harming British Columbia's coastal ecosystems. Accidentally introduced through the oyster trade, they are now harvested commercially and added to chowders, soups, and appetizers — to the delight of many seafood connoisseurs.'

narrativeText: The most poisonous mushrooms on Earth belong to the genus Amanita. The fly agaric, a potent hallucinogen, probably wouldn't kill you, but could make you extremely sick. In 18th-century Siberia, people would drink the urine of reindeer, or other humans, that had eaten this mushroom. This filtered out the other toxins, leaving the hallucinogen intact. The death cap mushroom may be what the Roman Agrippina used to kill her husband, Emperor Claudius.
notes: Overview photograph spanning multiple display panels. Visible panel code: D075. Upper section shows fly agaric and death cap mushroom specimens with narrative. Middle section includes a range map for Oregon spotted frog (Rana pretiosa, Abbotsford, BC), a genetics/DNA panel with vertical text about samples 'collected in Vancouver' discussing genetic diversity and family resemblance, a graph, and humpback cowrie shell specimens. Lower section has text: 'What is a species anyway? In biology, a species is often defined as a group of organisms, or genetic community, that interbreed and produce fertile offspring. Scientists use similarities and differences among organisms as clues to decide if they are different species.' Also visible: a duck-billed platypus specimen with text beginning 'Why do wh...' and a salamander/newt specimen.

narrativeText: Killer algae? Some microscopic algae produce potent toxins. When populations of these toxic algae explode in a "harmful algal bloom," sometimes called "red tide," shellfish may eat them and become poisonous themselves. People who consume contaminated shellfish can become paralyzed or even die. The Canadian government monitors the safety of our seafood to prevent shellfish poisoning from microorganisms such as these.

The invaders drunk so freely and unsuspectingly, that they were quickly overpowered and slaughtered by their treacherous foes." —K. Gascobridge, The Naturalist, 1859

narrativeText: Most of the squirrels you'll see in Vancouver are non-native eastern grey squirrels, all descended from a handful introduced to Stanley Park a century ago. A familiar aroma in Vancouver is the smell of skunk. They're carnivores, and normally eat grubs, mice, and eggs, but in the city, they're just as likely to go after your garbage. The end-Ordovician extinction consisted of two events between 450 and 440 million years ago, when all known life on Earth lived in the oceans. A large land mass drifted into the south polar region, making temperatures plummet, ice form, and sea levels fall, destroying ocean habitat. About two-thirds of marine invertebrates disappeared, including two-thirds of all brachiopod and... Some orcas eat higher on the food chain than others, preferring animals like seals and porpoises to salmon and squid. But this gourmet diet comes with a price: the mammals they eat deliver more concentrated doses of pollutants. orcas' bodies are toxic waste contaminated with herbicides, stain repellents, fire retardants, and detergents.
