narrativeText: Orchids, buttercups, lilies, and roses aren't just for the garden. In our local forests, plants from these families and dozens more in a myriad of colours and shapes, await the careful observer in spring and summer. Life's not always easy for mosses in the canopy. In summer, they have to be able to withstand both drying out and the leaves of their host tree blocking the sun. But in autumn, the leaves fall and the rains come, bringing sunlight and water so the moss can thrive again.

narrativeText: Conifer trees are the scaffolding of Vancouver's landscape. Our most common native tree is Douglas fir, which you can identify by its needles: they are attached individually instead of in bundles, and they spiral around the branches. Ferns and horsetails, like conifers, reproduce without flowers and are abundant in our woods. Most adult insects sport two pairs of wings. Notice how some wings are delicate and transparent, and others are solid and colourful. These and other differences can help you distinguish one insect group from another. True flies have one pair of wings.
notes: Large panoramic composite display. Thumbnail shows conifer/fern specimens with Douglas-fir cones and branches. narrativeText: Native edible plants abound in Vancouver. Huckleberries, salmonberries, and thimbleberries produce edible fruits, and Siberian miner's lettuce makes a great addition to salads. Even nettles are tasty when lightly cooked to disarm the sting! The Holocene extinction, beginning about 10,000 years ago... It's time to test your knowledge. Can you name the insect order that each one of these specimens represents? Answers: A. Beetle (Coleoptera) B. Katydid (Orthoptera) C. Termite (Isoptera) D. Earwig (Dermaptera) E. Bumblebee (Hymenoptera) F. Caddisfly (Trichoptera) G. Moth (Lepidoptera) H. Dragonfly (Odonata) I. Honeybee (Hymenoptera) J. Millipede (non-insect) K. House fly (Diptera) L. Lacewing (Neuroptera) M. Butterfly (Lepidoptera) N. Bug (Hemiptera) O. Beetle (Coleoptera) P. Moth (Lepidoptera)

narrativeText: Humans are just beginning to discover the thousands of useful chemicals plants contain. We know about their flavours, aromas, and healing properties... From ancient Greece and China to today... people have long used plants medicinally. Oregano on your pizza, but did you know that it has also been used to cure stomach and respiratory ailments? Unfortunately, not all plants actually... be harmful if used...
legibility: medium; circular mauve labels with rotated white text, some labels partially obscured by plant material
notes: Rod of Asclepius / caduceus symbol in center. 12 pressed plant specimens arranged around the symbol with circular mauve labels. All collected from UBC Botanical Garden, Vancouver, BC except E0591 and E0592 from August 10, 2011.

narrativeText: Milkweed is poisonous to animals, including people and most insects. Some insects, like milkweed bugs and monarch butterflies, have evolved not only to tolerate the plant's toxins, but to keep them in their bodies as a defence. The poison sickens predators, which soon learn to avoid the insects' bright colours. Humans have also found uses for milkweed. Some First Nations use the plant medicinally, including removing warts.
notes: Milkweed pressed specimen collected June 12, 2011, Murray Creek Road, BC. Small milkweed bug collected July 28-August 18, 1997, Chopaka, BC. Large milkweed bug collected January 23, 1955, UBC campus, Vancouver, BC. Two insect specimens in magnifying lens mounts. Monarch caterpillar shown in photo credited to Lisa Brown.

narrativeText: Strong aromas like pine, citrus, and mint all come from one group of plant chemicals, called terpenes, often found in tree bark. Many of these chemicals have been shown to ward off insects. Whatever its use to the plant, the terpene taxol, from the bark of Pacific yew, helps fight cancer. Taxol is now synthesized in the lab, sparing wild populations of Pacific yew.
notes: Taxus sp. collected August 8, 2011, UBC campus, Vancouver, BC (branch with foliage, photo sized 7.75x9in). Pacific yew bark collected August 7, 2011, Emerald Forest Conservation Area, Whistler, BC (bark pieces, photo sized ~7x8.25in). Close-up photo of yew berry credited to Derek Tan.

narrativeText: Plants in the genus Artemesia include sagebrush and wormwood (an ingredient in absinthe). Another species is the source of artemisinin, a potent anti-malarial drug. Used by the Chinese for over a thousand years, artemesinin was "discovered" internationally in the 1980s. Today, the chemical is so widely used that malarial parasites are developing resistance.
notes: Wormwood pressed specimen collected August 4, 2011, UBC Botanical Garden, Vancouver, BC. Close-up photo of wormwood in situ, photo credit partially legible (possibly David Gilbar). Note: narrative text spells genus as 'Artemesia' (variant spelling of Artemisia).

narrativeText: Can a plant lead to war? When the Chinese restricted imports of British goods, the British East India Company balanced the trade deficit by selling opium, grown in India, to Chinese merchants and smugglers. Chinese resistance to the trade led to the 19th-century Opium Wars, and the economic effects of centuries of opium addiction paved the way for the rise of Chinese Communism. Ornamental varieties of opium poppy, low in opiates, are now commonly grown in gardens.
notes: Multi-panel interpretive display about plant reproduction and ethnobotany. Top portion shows opium poppy narrative with Battle of Palikao (1860) illustration. Lower panels show Douglas-fir seed cones with life cycle diagram (Seedling, Mature plant, Pollen cones, Flowers, Fertilization, Seeds), garden pea with flower anatomy (Stigma, Pollen, Stamen, Ovary, Egg, Ovule) and life cycle, and pressed flowering plants on purple circles. narrativeText: Used for hundreds of years to treat heart troubles, foxglove was the original source of digitalis, a heart medicine now synthesized in factories. But digitalis is also a potent poison, and eating any part of the foxglove plant can be fatal. Digitalis is a glycoside, one of the most important kinds of plant poisons (and medicines).
notes: Pressed foxglove specimen collected August 3, 2011, Pacific Spirit Park, Vancouver, BC. Close-up photo of foxglove flower credited to Kristy Robbins.
